tagline: An honest, unhurried library for autoimmune readers.

articles.title: What actually causes autoimmune disease
articles.hero_alt: What actually causes autoimmune disease (illustration)
articles.author: The Immune Rebuilt Editorial Team
articles.excerpt: Most patients arrive at this question already exhausted by the headline answers: an unhurried, honest look at autoimmune root causes. The science is messier than the headlines, and that mess is where the answers live. Genes load the gun, environment pulls the trigger, and the bod
articles.body:
TL;DR. the headline answer the literature actually gives; the four classes of trigger that show up over and over; why the same trigger does not produce the same disease in two people; what this means for the people living inside it; where to begin if you are at the start of this. The point is to read this slowly, compare it against your own story, and pick one small move for this week.

By The Immune Rebuilt Editorial Team for Immune Rebuilt . April 1, 2026

Most patients arrive at this question already exhausted by the headline answers, but the answer worth giving in plain English is rarely the one that gets the most clicks. This piece is part of Immune Rebuilt library on autoimmune disease, and like the rest of the library it tries to be specific, unhurried, and honest about what we do and do not know. The science is messier than the headlines, and that mess is where the answers live. Genes load the gun, environment pulls the trigger, and the body keeps the score.

If you are coming to this fresh, you may also want the wider frame in our piece on leaky gut without the hype, which sets out the lens we use across this site, including molecular mimicry explained, before we narrow the field to root causes.

The headline answer the literature actually gives

It helps to start by stating the question precisely. When patients ask about autoimmune root causes, they usually mean a cluster of three things at once: what is happening in the body, what is causing it, and what is realistic to do about it on a normal week. The literature usually answers only one of those at a time, which is why even careful reading can leave a person feeling unsatisfied. The frame Immune Rebuilt returns to is the multi-hit picture: genetic load, environmental exposure, immune history, and lived stress, with each contributing some of the variance and none of them sufficient on their own. That frame is not exciting, but it is durable, and it tends to age well as new evidence comes in. We will keep that frame here, and we will be specific about what is well-supported, what is plausible, and what is still being argued. If you want the wider scaffold this piece is sitting on, our overview at leaky gut without the hype pairs neatly with this one and is a fine place to begin.

The four classes of trigger that show up over and over

The next layer is the mechanism, in plain English. The science is messier than the headlines, and that mess is where the answers live. Genes load the gun, environment pulls the trigger, and the body keeps the score. The cleanest way to see this in root causes is to follow the cells and the signals, not the slogans. The immune system is not a single switch; it is a choir of overlapping populations, regulated by short-lived signalling molecules and long-lived training effects. Anything that biases that choir for long enough can become a contributor to disease activity. That includes obvious inputs like food and infection, and less obvious ones like sleep architecture, trauma history, and exposure to indoor air quality. None of these are mystical; they are just unevenly distributed across patient populations, which is part of why the same intervention works strikingly well for some people and not at all for others. We try to keep that variance visible in everything Immune Rebuilt publishes, and our piece on molecular mimicry explained is one of the better worked examples of this in our library.

Why the same trigger does not produce the same disease in two people

It is worth pausing on what the evidence base actually supports. The strongest claims for autoimmune root causes come from prospective cohort data and well-conducted small randomized trials, neither of which is glamorous and both of which are easy to misread when they reach the consumer internet. A finding that holds up across two or three independent cohorts, with a believable mechanism behind it and a meaningful effect size on real outcomes, is the bar this site tries to use. By that standard, several of the interventions usually grouped under root causes earn their slot, and several do not. We try to say which is which without scolding either side. If you want to see how that plays out in another corner of the library, ebv and autoimmunity is a useful next read because it walks the same evidence ladder for a different question.

What this means for the people living inside it

Practical implementation is the part where most autoimmune reading goes wrong, and not because the reader is doing anything wrong. The information environment is set up to reward intensity and punish steadiness, and autoimmune root causes is a place where intensity is mostly counterproductive. The patients who do best with this material tend to make small, specific changes, hold them for long enough to see what they actually did, and only then add the next layer. That is much harder to package and sell than a thirty-day transformation, but it matches what works in clinic. A useful rule of thumb is to change one thing per two weeks, write three lines a night about how it felt, and review on Sundays in five minutes. That is the shape of careful work, and Immune Rebuilt tries to model it everywhere on the site. The companion piece stress and the hpa axis sits next door to this one and is a natural follow-on if this section landed.

Where to begin if you are at the start of this

Finally, fit and pacing. Not every autoimmune reader is in the same week of the same year. Some come to autoimmune root causes in the middle of an active flare, where the priority is to soften, sleep, and stop adding variables. Some come in a long quiet stretch where they have the bandwidth to run a real experiment. Some are post-diagnosis and still grieving; some have been doing this for fifteen years and are tired of being lectured to. The lens this site uses is gentle but specific: take the smallest believable step, hold it long enough for it to mean something, and protect your sleep, your nervous system, and your friendships on the way. That last one is not a throwaway. Autoimmune disease is uniquely capable of crowding out the relationships that quietly hold a life together, and reclaiming that ground is part of the work. The library at Immune Rebuilt is built to support that pace, and the related-reading list at the bottom of this piece is a way of staying inside that conversation rather than chasing every loud headline.

Where to read more

For readers who want to go deeper than this article, National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases overview of autoimmune disease is a useful next stop: https://www.niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/autoimmune-diseases. It is intentionally not a wellness blog, and we link it because the underlying evidence is what informs the position Immune Rebuilt takes on this topic.

Related reading on this site

If this piece resonated, the most useful neighbours are probably leaky gut without the hype, molecular mimicry explained, and ebv and autoimmunity; and if you are a long-haul autoimmune reader, stress and the hpa axis sits in the same neighbourhood and is worth the half-hour.

Common questions, answered plainly

Three questions arrive in our inbox every time we publish on autoimmune root causes. Is this real, or is it overstated? The answer is usually both: the underlying biology is real, the consumer-internet version is overstated, and the careful middle is where useful decisions live. Where do I begin? Begin with sleep, food quality, and one nervous-system practice you can sustain for a month, before adding anything else. leaky gut without the hype and molecular mimicry explained both walk through this in detail. How long until I should expect to notice anything? For most autoimmune readers the early signal is two to four weeks, the durable signal is three to six months, and the meaningful labs usually take longer than that. Patience is not a personality trait here; it is part of the protocol.

A fourth question turns up nearly as often: am I doing this wrong if I cannot do all of it? No one does all of it. The patients who do best with autoimmune root causes are the ones who pick the two or three changes that fit their life, hold them long enough to see the effect, and let the rest of the list be aspirational rather than urgent. That is the principle behind every piece in Immune Rebuilt library, and it is the reason ebv and autoimmunity sits next to this one in our recommended reading list. If you take only one sentence from this article, take this one: small, specific, sustained, kind to your own pacing.

What to do this week

Pick the smallest believable move from this piece. Run it for seven days. Write three honest lines in a notebook each evening. That is the entire homework. On day seven, read those lines back without trying to score them; the pattern that emerges is the data. Most autoimmune progress is made of weeks like this one, not of dramatic resets, and the literature on behaviour change agrees with the clinical reality on this point. Immune Rebuilt exists to make this kind of small, careful work feel like it is allowed, because in autoimmune medicine the small careful work is almost always the work that holds. Root causes are not a single dragon to slay; they are weather to learn. Read this site as a long quiet conversation about that weather, not as a checklist.

If you are reading this in a flare, please be kind to the version of you that opened this tab. Bookmark the piece, close the laptop, and come back when the room feels less loud. The library at Immune Rebuilt will still be here, and so will the rest of your life.

TL;DR. why aip exists, and what problem it is actually trying to solve; what the diet actually looks like, in plain english; the evidence base, summarized fairly; how to do aip without it taking over your life; reintroduction, the part most people skip. By The Immune Rebuilt Editorial Team for Immune Rebuilt . April 3, 2026

This piece is part of Immune Rebuilt library on autoimmune disease, and like the rest of the library it tries to be specific, unhurried, and honest about what we do and do not know. AIP is a tool. Used well, it is one of the most clarifying short-term experiments an autoimmune patient can run. Used poorly, it becomes another long shadow.

If you are coming to this fresh, you may also want the wider frame in our piece on leaky gut without the hype, which sets out the lens we use across this site, including elimination diet reintroduction, before we narrow the field to aip & diet.

articles.excerpt: On the days when nothing else seems to land, this is the lens that still earns its keep: an unhurried, honest look at Hashimoto thyroiditis as a multi-system autoimmune story. Hashimoto's is rarely an isolated event; it is more often the visible edge of a wider immune story that
articles.body:
TL;DR. why tpo antibodies show up before tsh does anything; triggers that recur in hashimoto's case histories; standard care versus integrative care, fairly compared; what the research actually shows about diet; living with it without making it your identity. By The Immune Rebuilt Editorial Team for Immune Rebuilt . April 5, 2026

Hashimoto's is rarely an isolated event; it is more often the visible edge of a wider immune story that began long before the TSH did anything obvious.

If you are coming to this fresh, you may also want the wider frame in our piece on selenium and thyroid antibodies, which sets out the lens we use across this site, including leaky gut without the hype, before we narrow the field to conditions.

Why TPO antibodies show up before TSH does anything

It helps to start by stating the question precisely. When patients ask about Hashimoto thyroiditis as a multi-system autoimmune story, they usually mean a cluster of three things at once: what is happening in the body, what is causing it, and what is realistic to do about it on a normal week. The literature usually answers only one of those at a time, which is why even careful reading can leave a person feeling unsatisfied. The frame Immune Rebuilt returns to is the multi-hit picture: genetic load, environmental exposure, immune history, and lived stress, with each contributing some of the variance and none of them sufficient on their own. That frame is not exciting, but it is durable, and it tends to age well as new evidence comes in. We will keep that frame here, and we will be specific about what is well-supported, what is plausible, and what is still being argued. If you want the wider scaffold this piece is sitting on, our overview at selenium and thyroid antibodies pairs neatly with this one and is a fine place to begin.

Triggers that recur in Hashimoto's case histories

The next layer is the mechanism, in plain English. Hashimoto's is rarely an isolated event; it is more often the visible edge of a wider immune story that began long before the TSH did anything obvious. The cleanest way to see this in conditions is to follow the cells and the signals, not the slogans. The immune system is not a single switch; it is a choir of overlapping populations, regulated by short-lived signalling molecules and long-lived training effects. Anything that biases that choir for long enough can become a contributor to disease activity. That includes obvious inputs like food and infection, and less obvious ones like sleep architecture, trauma history, and exposure to indoor air quality. None of these are mystical; they are just unevenly distributed across patient populations, which is part of why the same intervention works strikingly well for some people and not at all for others. We try to keep that variance visible in everything Immune Rebuilt publishes, and our piece on leaky gut without the hype is one of the better worked examples of this in our library.

Standard care versus integrative care, fairly compared

It is worth pausing on what the evidence base actually supports. The strongest claims for Hashimoto thyroiditis as a multi-system autoimmune story come from prospective cohort data and well-conducted small randomized trials, neither of which is glamorous and both of which are easy to misread when they reach the consumer internet. A finding that holds up across two or three independent cohorts, with a believable mechanism behind it and a meaningful effect size on real outcomes, is the bar this site tries to use. By that standard, several of the interventions usually grouped under conditions earn their slot, and several do not. We try to say which is which without scolding either side. If you want to see how that plays out in another corner of the library, molecular mimicry explained is a useful next read because it walks the same evidence ladder for a different question.

What the research actually shows about diet

Practical implementation is the part where most autoimmune reading goes wrong, and not because the reader is doing anything wrong. The information environment is set up to reward intensity and punish steadiness, and Hashimoto thyroiditis as a multi-system autoimmune story is a place where intensity is mostly counterproductive. The patients who do best with this material tend to make small, specific changes, hold them for long enough to see what they actually did, and only then add the next layer. That is much harder to package and sell than a thirty-day transformation, but it matches what works in clinic. A useful rule of thumb is to change one thing per two weeks, write three lines a night about how it felt, and review on Sundays in five minutes. Where the Wahls protocol came from

It helps to start by stating the question precisely. When patients ask about Wahls protocol, mitochondrial nutrition, MS, they usually mean a cluster of three things at once: what is happening in the body, what is causing it, and what is realistic to do about it on a normal week. The literature usually answers only one of those at a time, which is why even careful reading can leave a person feeling unsatisfied. The plate, in concrete terms

The next layer is the mechanism, in plain English. Terry Wahls' protocol overlaps with AIP and adds a mitochondrial frame. The trial evidence is small but real; the practical core is unusually doable. The cleanest way to see this in aip & diet is to follow the cells and the signals, not the slogans. Anything that biases that choir for long enough can become a contributor to disease activity. None of these are mystical; they are just unevenly distributed across patient populations, which is part of why the same intervention works strikingly well for some people and not at all for others. We try to keep that variance visible in everything Immune Rebuilt publishes, and our piece on mitochondria and autoimmune fatigue is one of the better worked examples of this in our library.

What the trials actually show

It is worth pausing on what the evidence base actually supports. articles.title: Selenium and thyroid antibodies, what the trials actually say
articles.hero_alt: Selenium and thyroid antibodies, what the trials actually say (illustration)
articles.author: The Immune Rebuilt Editorial Team
articles.excerpt: There is a way to say this that is both gentle and specific, which is the only way worth saying it: an unhurried, honest look at selenium supplementation in Hashimoto thyroiditis. Selenium has more autoimmune trial data than almost any other single nutrient; the right reading is
articles.body:
TL;DR. what selenium does in thyroid biology; what the antibody trials actually show; why food first is not romantic, it is practical; where supplementation can earn the slot; where it overshoots. By The Immune Rebuilt Editorial Team for Immune Rebuilt . April 12, 2026

This piece is part of Immune Rebuilt library on autoimmune disease, and like the rest of the library it tries to be specific, unhurried, and honest about what we do and do not know. Selenium has more autoimmune trial data than almost any other single nutrient; the right reading is more careful than 'take 200 mcg and your antibodies fall'.

If you are coming to this fresh, you may also want the wider frame in our piece on hashimotos thyroid and the autoimmune mosaic, which sets out the lens we use across this site, including supplement essentials for autoimmune, before we narrow the field to conditions.

What selenium does in thyroid biology

It helps to start by stating the question precisely. When patients ask about selenium supplementation in Hashimoto thyroiditis, they usually mean a cluster of three things at once: what is happening in the body, what is causing it, and what is realistic to do about it on a normal week. The literature usually answers only one of those at a time, which is why even careful reading can leave a person feeling unsatisfied. The frame Immune Rebuilt returns to is the multi-hit picture: genetic load, environmental exposure, immune history, and lived stress, with each contributing some of the variance and none of them sufficient on their own. That frame is not exciting, but it is durable, and it tends to age well as new evidence comes in. We will keep that frame here, and we will be specific about what is well-supported, what is plausible, and what is still being argued. If you want the wider scaffold this piece is sitting on, our overview at hashimotos thyroid and the autoimmune mosaic pairs neatly with this one and is a fine place to begin.

What the antibody trials actually show

The next layer is the mechanism, in plain English. Selenium has more autoimmune trial data than almost any other single nutrient; the right reading is more careful than 'take 200 mcg and your antibodies fall'. The cleanest way to see this in conditions is to follow the cells and the signals, not the slogans. The immune system is not a single switch; it is a choir of overlapping populations, regulated by short-lived signalling molecules and long-lived training effects. Anything that biases that choir for long enough can become a contributor to disease activity. That includes obvious inputs like food and infection, and less obvious ones like sleep architecture, trauma history, and exposure to indoor air quality. None of these are mystical; they are just unevenly distributed across patient populations, which is part of why the same intervention works strikingly well for some people and not at all for others. We try to keep that variance visible in everything Immune Rebuilt publishes, and our piece on supplement essentials for autoimmune is one of the better worked examples of this in our library.

Why food first is not romantic, it is practical

It is worth pausing on what the evidence base actually supports. The strongest claims for selenium supplementation in Hashimoto thyroiditis come from prospective cohort data and well-conducted small randomized trials, neither of which is glamorous and both of which are easy to misread when they reach the consumer internet. A finding that holds up across two or three independent cohorts, with a believable mechanism behind it and a meaningful effect size on real outcomes, is the bar this site tries to use. By that standard, several of the interventions usually grouped under conditions earn their slot, and several do not. We try to say which is which without scolding either side. If you want to see how that plays out in another corner of the library, functional medicine and aip is a useful next read because it walks the same evidence ladder for a different question.

Where supplementation can earn the slot

Practical implementation is the part where most autoimmune reading goes wrong, and not because the reader is doing anything wrong. The information environment is set up to reward intensity and punish steadiness, and selenium supplementation in Hashimoto thyroiditis is a place where intensity is mostly counterproductive. The patients who do best with this material tend to make small, specific changes, hold them for long enough to see what they actually did, and only then add the next layer. That is much harder to package and sell than a thirty-day transformation, but it matches what works in clinic. A useful rule of thumb is to change one thing per two weeks, write three lines a night about how it felt, and review on Sundays in five minutes. That is the shape of careful work, and Immune Rebuilt tries to model it everywhere on the site. The companion piece food sensitivity testing honest sits next door to this one and is a natural follow-on if this section landed.

Where it overshoots

Finally, fit and pacing. Not every autoimmune reader is in the same week of the same year. Some come to selenium supplementation in Hashimoto thyroiditis in the middle of an active flare, where the priority is to soften, sleep, and stop adding variables. Some come in a long quiet stretch where they have the bandwidth to run a real experiment. Some are post-diagnosis and still grieving; some have been doing this for fifteen years and are tired of being lectured to. The lens this site uses is gentle but specific: take the smallest believable step, hold it long enough for it to mean something, and protect your sleep, your nervous system, and your friendships on the way. That last one is not a throwaway. Autoimmune disease is uniquely capable of crowding out the relationships that quietly hold a life together, and reclaiming that ground is part of the work. The library at Immune Rebuilt is built to support that pace, and the related-reading list at the bottom of this piece is a way of staying inside that conversation rather than chasing every loud headline.

It is intentionally not a wellness blog, and we link it because the underlying evidence is what informs the position Immune Rebuilt takes on this topic.

Where food earns its slot

Practical implementation is the part where most autoimmune reading goes wrong, and not because the reader is doing anything wrong. The information environment is set up to reward intensity and punish steadiness, and systemic lupus erythematosus, flare triggers, gentle pacing is a place where intensity is mostly counterproductive. The patients who do best with this material tend to make small, specific changes, hold them for long enough to see what they actually did, and only then add the next layer. That is the shape of careful work, and Immune Rebuilt tries to model it everywhere on the site. Finally, fit and pacing. Not every autoimmune reader is in the same week of the same year. Some come to systemic lupus erythematosus, flare triggers, gentle pacing in the middle of an active flare, where the priority is to soften, sleep, and stop adding variables. Some come in a long quiet stretch where they have the bandwidth to run a real experiment. Some are post-diagnosis and still grieving; some have been doing this for fifteen years and are tired of being lectured to. The lens this site uses is gentle but specific: take the smallest believable step, hold it long enough for it to mean something, and protect your sleep, your nervous system, and your friendships on the way. That last one is not a throwaway. Autoimmune disease is uniquely capable of crowding out the relationships that quietly hold a life together, and reclaiming that ground is part of the work. Pick the smallest believable move from this piece. Run it for seven days. Write three honest lines in a notebook each evening. That is the entire homework. articles.body:
TL;DR. what an igg panel actually measures; why the aaaai position statement matters; where these panels can still be a useful starting point; what outperforms the panel almost every time; how to spend the test fee better. By The Immune Rebuilt Editorial Team for Immune Rebuilt . April 19, 2026

Test results are useful only when they change a decision, and a surprising number of them never quite do, but the answer worth giving in plain English is rarely the one that gets the most clicks. This piece is part of Immune Rebuilt library on autoimmune disease, and like the rest of the library it tries to be specific, unhurried, and honest about what we do and do not know. IgG food panels are popular for reasons that have nothing to do with how well they predict outcomes; what they actually measure is mostly exposure, not pathology.

If you are coming to this fresh, you may also want the wider frame in our piece on functional medicine and aip, which sets out the lens we use across this site, including elimination diet reintroduction, before we narrow the field to functional medicine.

What an IgG panel actually measures

It helps to start by stating the question precisely. When patients ask about IgG and other food sensitivity panels critically reviewed, they usually mean a cluster of three things at once: what is happening in the body, what is causing it, and what is realistic to do about it on a normal week. The literature usually answers only one of those at a time, which is why even careful reading can leave a person feeling unsatisfied. The frame Immune Rebuilt returns to is the multi-hit picture: genetic load, environmental exposure, immune history, and lived stress, with each contributing some of the variance and none of them sufficient on their own. That frame is not exciting, but it is durable, and it tends to age well as new evidence comes in. We will keep that frame here, and we will be specific about what is well-supported, what is plausible, and what is still being argued. If you want the wider scaffold this piece is sitting on, our overview at functional medicine and aip pairs neatly with this one and is a fine place to begin.

Why the AAAAI position statement matters

The next layer is the mechanism, in plain English. IgG food panels are popular for reasons that have nothing to do with how well they predict outcomes; what they actually measure is mostly exposure, not pathology. The cleanest way to see this in functional medicine is to follow the cells and the signals, not the slogans. The immune system is not a single switch; it is a choir of overlapping populations, regulated by short-lived signalling molecules and long-lived training effects. Anything that biases that choir for long enough can become a contributor to disease activity. That includes obvious inputs like food and infection, and less obvious ones like sleep architecture, trauma history, and exposure to indoor air quality. None of these are mystical; they are just unevenly distributed across patient populations, which is part of why the same intervention works strikingly well for some people and not at all for others. We try to keep that variance visible in everything Immune Rebuilt publishes, and our piece on elimination diet reintroduction is one of the better worked examples of this in our library.

Where these panels can still be a useful starting point

It is worth pausing on what the evidence base actually supports. The strongest claims for IgG and other food sensitivity panels critically reviewed come from prospective cohort data and well-conducted small randomized trials, neither of which is glamorous and both of which are easy to misread when they reach the consumer internet. A finding that holds up across two or three independent cohorts, with a believable mechanism behind it and a meaningful effect size on real outcomes, is the bar this site tries to use. By that standard, several of the interventions usually grouped under functional medicine earn their slot, and several do not. We try to say which is which without scolding either side. If you want to see how that plays out in another corner of the library, aip protocol honest guide is a useful next read because it walks the same evidence ladder for a different question.

What outperforms the panel almost every time

Practical implementation is the part where most autoimmune reading goes wrong, and not because the reader is doing anything wrong. The information environment is set up to reward intensity and punish steadiness, and IgG and other food sensitivity panels critically reviewed is a place where intensity is mostly counterproductive. The patients who do best with this material tend to make small, specific changes, hold them for long enough to see what they actually did, and only then add the next layer. That is much harder to package and sell than a thirty-day transformation, but it matches what works in clinic. A useful rule of thumb is to change one thing per two weeks, write three lines a night about how it felt, and review on Sundays in five minutes. That is the shape of careful work, and Immune Rebuilt tries to model it everywhere on the site. The companion piece gi map and stool testing sits next door to this one and is a natural follow-on if this section landed.

How to spend the test fee better

Finally, fit and pacing. Not every autoimmune reader is in the same week of the same year. Some come to IgG and other food sensitivity panels critically reviewed in the middle of an active flare, where the priority is to soften, sleep, and stop adding variables. Some come in a long quiet stretch where they have the bandwidth to run a real experiment. Some are post-diagnosis and still grieving; some have been doing this for fifteen years and are tired of being lectured to. The lens this site uses is gentle but specific: take the smallest believable step, hold it long enough for it to mean something, and protect your sleep, your nervous system, and your friendships on the way. That last one is not a throwaway. Autoimmune disease is uniquely capable of crowding out the relationships that quietly hold a life together, and reclaiming that ground is part of the work. It is intentionally not a wellness blog, and we link it because the underlying evidence is what informs the position Immune Rebuilt takes on this topic.

This piece is part of Immune Rebuilt library on autoimmune disease, and like the rest of the library it tries to be specific, unhurried, and honest about what we do and do not know. Some autoimmune patients are quietly being made sick by their building. The science is messier than the marketing, and the honest answer sits squarely in the careful middle.

If you are coming to this fresh, you may also want the wider frame in our piece on what actually causes autoimmune disease, which sets out the lens we use across this site, including gi map and stool testing, before we narrow the field to root causes.

Why this conversation is not woo, and not all signal either

It helps to start by stating the question precisely. When patients ask about mycotoxin and indoor mold exposure as autoimmune drivers, they usually mean a cluster of three things at once: what is happening in the body, what is causing it, and what is realistic to do about it on a normal week. The literature usually answers only one of those at a time, which is why even careful reading can leave a person feeling unsatisfied. The frame Immune Rebuilt returns to is the multi-hit picture: genetic load, environmental exposure, immune history, and lived stress, with each contributing some of the variance and none of them sufficient on their own. That frame is not exciting, but it is durable, and it tends to age well as new evidence comes in. We will keep that frame here, and we will be specific about what is well-supported, what is plausible, and what is still being argued. If you want the wider scaffold this piece is sitting on, our overview at what actually causes autoimmune disease pairs neatly with this one and is a fine place to begin.

How exposure actually drives immune dysregulation

The next layer is the mechanism, in plain English. Some autoimmune patients are quietly being made sick by their building. The science is messier than the marketing, and the honest answer sits squarely in the careful middle. The cleanest way to see this in root causes is to follow the cells and the signals, not the slogans. The immune system is not a single switch; it is a choir of overlapping populations, regulated by short-lived signalling molecules and long-lived training effects. Anything that biases that choir for long enough can become a contributor to disease activity. That includes obvious inputs like food and infection, and less obvious ones like sleep architecture, trauma history, and exposure to indoor air quality. None of these are mystical; they are just unevenly distributed across patient populations, which is part of why the same intervention works strikingly well for some people and not at all for others. We try to keep that variance visible in everything Immune Rebuilt publishes, and our piece on gi map and stool testing is one of the better worked examples of this in our library.

What the testing landscape looks like

It is worth pausing on what the evidence base actually supports. The strongest claims for mycotoxin and indoor mold exposure as autoimmune drivers come from prospective cohort data and well-conducted small randomized trials, neither of which is glamorous and both of which are easy to misread when they reach the consumer internet. A finding that holds up across two or three independent cohorts, with a believable mechanism behind it and a meaningful effect size on real outcomes, is the bar this site tries to use. By that standard, several of the interventions usually grouped under root causes earn their slot, and several do not. We try to say which is which without scolding either side. If you want to see how that plays out in another corner of the library, functional medicine and aip is a useful next read because it walks the same evidence ladder for a different question.

How to remediate without going broke

Practical implementation is the part where most autoimmune reading goes wrong, and not because the reader is doing anything wrong. The information environment is set up to reward intensity and punish steadiness, and mycotoxin and indoor mold exposure as autoimmune drivers is a place where intensity is mostly counterproductive. The patients who do best with this material tend to make small, specific changes, hold them for long enough to see what they actually did, and only then add the next layer. That is much harder to package and sell than a thirty-day transformation, but it matches what works in clinic. A useful rule of thumb is to change one thing per two weeks, write three lines a night about how it felt, and review on Sundays in five minutes. That is the shape of careful work, and Immune Rebuilt tries to model it everywhere on the site. The companion piece supplement essentials for autoimmune sits next door to this one and is a natural follow-on if this section landed.

When to leave a building

Finally, fit and pacing. Not every autoimmune reader is in the same week of the same year. Some come to mycotoxin and indoor mold exposure as autoimmune drivers in the middle of an active flare, where the priority is to soften, sleep, and stop adding variables. Some come in a long quiet stretch where they have the bandwidth to run a real experiment. Some are post-diagnosis and still grieving; some have been doing this for fifteen years and are tired of being lectured to. The lens this site uses is gentle but specific: take the smallest believable step, hold it long enough for it to mean something, and protect your sleep, your nervous system, and your friendships on the way. That last one is not a throwaway. Autoimmune disease is uniquely capable of crowding out the relationships that quietly hold a life together, and reclaiming that ground is part of the work. The library at Immune Rebuilt is built to support that pace, and the related-reading list at the bottom of this piece is a way of staying inside that conversation rather than chasing every loud headline.

The patients who do best with mycotoxin and indoor mold exposure as autoimmune drivers are the ones who pick the two or three changes that fit their life, hold them long enough to see the effect, and let the rest of the list be aspirational rather than urgent. What to do this week

Pick the smallest believable move from this piece. Run it for seven days. Write three honest lines in a notebook each evening. That is the entire homework. On day seven, read those lines back without trying to score them; the pattern that emerges is the data. Most autoimmune progress is made of weeks like this one, not of dramatic resets, and the literature on behaviour change agrees with the clinical reality on this point. Immune Rebuilt exists to make this kind of small, careful work feel like it is allowed, because in autoimmune medicine the small careful work is almost always the work that holds. Root causes are not a single dragon to slay; they are weather to learn. Read this site as a long quiet conversation about that weather, not as a checklist.

Related reading on this site

articles.title: Endometriosis, immune dysregulation, and the autoimmune-adjacent story
articles.hero_alt: Endometriosis, immune dysregulation, and the autoimmune-adjacent story (illustration)
articles.author: The Immune Rebuilt Editorial Team
articles.excerpt: Endometriosis sits awkwardly between specialties, which is precisely why the autoimmune lens helps: an unhurried, honest look at endometriosis as an immune story. Endometriosis is increasingly framed as immune-dysregulation-driven; the parallels with autoimmune patients are strik
articles.body:
TL;DR. why endometriosis keeps appearing alongside autoimmune; estrogen, immune signalling, and the gut; anti-inflammatory food, fairly; pelvic floor, vagal tone, and somatic care; care across silos. The point is to read this slowly, compare it against your own story, and pick one small move for this week.

By The Immune Rebuilt Editorial Team for Immune Rebuilt . April 30, 2026

Endometriosis sits awkwardly between specialties, which is precisely why the autoimmune lens helps, but the answer worth giving in plain English is rarely the one that gets the most clicks. This piece is part of Immune Rebuilt library on autoimmune disease, and like the rest of the library it tries to be specific, unhurried, and honest about what we do and do not know. Endometriosis is increasingly framed as immune-dysregulation-driven; the parallels with autoimmune patients are striking, and the principles overlap more than care silos suggest.

If you are coming to this fresh, you may also want the wider frame in our piece on leaky gut without the hype, which sets out the lens we use across this site, including stress and the hpa axis, before we narrow the field to conditions.

Why endometriosis keeps appearing alongside autoimmune

It helps to start by stating the question precisely. When patients ask about endometriosis as an immune story, they usually mean a cluster of three things at once: what is happening in the body, what is causing it, and what is realistic to do about it on a normal week. The literature usually answers only one of those at a time, which is why even careful reading can leave a person feeling unsatisfied. The frame Immune Rebuilt returns to is the multi-hit picture: genetic load, environmental exposure, immune history, and lived stress, with each contributing some of the variance and none of them sufficient on their own. That frame is not exciting, but it is durable, and it tends to age well as new evidence comes in. We will keep that frame here, and we will be specific about what is well-supported, what is plausible, and what is still being argued. If you want the wider scaffold this piece is sitting on, our overview at leaky gut without the hype pairs neatly with this one and is a fine place to begin.

Estrogen, immune signalling, and the gut

The next layer is the mechanism, in plain English. Endometriosis is increasingly framed as immune-dysregulation-driven; the parallels with autoimmune patients are striking, and the principles overlap more than care silos suggest. The cleanest way to see this in conditions is to follow the cells and the signals, not the slogans. The immune system is not a single switch; it is a choir of overlapping populations, regulated by short-lived signalling molecules and long-lived training effects. Anything that biases that choir for long enough can become a contributor to disease activity. That includes obvious inputs like food and infection, and less obvious ones like sleep architecture, trauma history, and exposure to indoor air quality. None of these are mystical; they are just unevenly distributed across patient populations, which is part of why the same intervention works strikingly well for some people and not at all for others. We try to keep that variance visible in everything Immune Rebuilt publishes, and our piece on stress and the hpa axis is one of the better worked examples of this in our library.

Anti-inflammatory food, fairly

It is worth pausing on what the evidence base actually supports. The strongest claims for endometriosis as an immune story come from prospective cohort data and well-conducted small randomized trials, neither of which is glamorous and both of which are easy to misread when they reach the consumer internet. A finding that holds up across two or three independent cohorts, with a believable mechanism behind it and a meaningful effect size on real outcomes, is the bar this site tries to use. By that standard, several of the interventions usually grouped under conditions earn their slot, and several do not. We try to say which is which without scolding either side. If you want to see how that plays out in another corner of the library, emotional roots essay is a useful next read because it walks the same evidence ladder for a different question.

Pelvic floor, vagal tone, and somatic care

Practical implementation is the part where most autoimmune reading goes wrong, and not because the reader is doing anything wrong. The information environment is set up to reward intensity and punish steadiness, and endometriosis as an immune story is a place where intensity is mostly counterproductive. The patients who do best with this material tend to make small, specific changes, hold them for long enough to see what they actually did, and only then add the next layer. That is much harder to package and sell than a thirty-day transformation, but it matches what works in clinic. A useful rule of thumb is to change one thing per two weeks, write three lines a night about how it felt, and review on Sundays in five minutes. That is the shape of careful work, and Immune Rebuilt tries to model it everywhere on the site. The companion piece supplement essentials for autoimmune sits next door to this one and is a natural follow-on if this section landed.

Care across silos

Finally, fit and pacing. Not every autoimmune reader is in the same week of the same year. Some come to endometriosis as an immune story in the middle of an active flare, where the priority is to soften, sleep, and stop adding variables. Some come in a long quiet stretch where they have the bandwidth to run a real experiment. Some are post-diagnosis and still grieving; some have been doing this for fifteen years and are tired of being lectured to. The lens this site uses is gentle but specific: take the smallest believable step, hold it long enough for it to mean something, and protect your sleep, your nervous system, and your friendships on the way. That last one is not a throwaway. Autoimmune disease is uniquely capable of crowding out the relationships that quietly hold a life together, and reclaiming that ground is part of the work. The library at Immune Rebuilt is built to support that pace, and the related-reading list at the bottom of this piece is a way of staying inside that conversation rather than chasing every loud headline.

Where to read more

For readers who want to go deeper than this article, an Endometriosis Foundation of America overview of immune research is a useful next stop: https://www.endofound.org/research. It is intentionally not a wellness blog, and we link it because the underlying evidence is what informs the position Immune Rebuilt takes on this topic.

Related reading on this site

If this piece resonated, the most useful neighbours are probably leaky gut without the hype, stress and the hpa axis, and emotional roots essay; and if you are a long-haul autoimmune reader, supplement essentials for autoimmune sits in the same neighbourhood and is worth the half-hour.

articles.title: Genes load the gun, environment pulls the trigger
articles.excerpt: What twin studies actually show, and why your environment carries more weight than your DNA.
articles.body:
TL;DR. how this question is usually asked, and why that matters; the honest mechanism, in plain english; where the same trigger lands differently in different bodies; common questions, answered plainly; where this fits into the wider picture; a small, kind move you can make today. The point is to read this slowly, compare it against your own story, and pick one small, kind move for this week.

By The Immune Rebuilt Editorial Team for Immune Rebuilt . May 2, 2026

It is fair to say very few questions inside autoimmune medicine attract more advice and less clarity than the question of cause, but the answer worth giving in plain English is rarely the one that gets the most clicks. This is part of the Immune Rebuilt library on autoimmune disease, and it tries to be specific, unhurried, and honest about what we do and do not know. What twin studies actually show, and why your environment carries more weight than your DNA.

If you are coming to this fresh, you may also want the wider frame in our piece on somatic practices for flare days, which sets out the lens this site uses across the library, including gi map and stool testing and perimenopause and autoimmune flares, before we narrow the field to root causes.

How this question is usually asked, and why that matters

The shape of the question really does matter. If we ask, with some impatience, what is the one cause of autoimmune disease, the literature shrugs at us, because there is not one. If we ask, instead, what are the recurring environmental and biological loads that, layered on top of a susceptible body, make autoimmune disease more likely, the literature offers a long, careful answer. The first question gets a quick fight on the internet. The second question gets a slow conversation that is, in our experience, where the actual recovery happens. The angle for this piece, what twin studies actually show, and why your environment carries more weight than your DNA, sits inside that second question; the first one is too small a doorway to walk through.

The honest mechanism, in plain English

Mechanism, in plain English, looks like this. In the case of genetic load vs environment, that machinery is specifically about what twin studies actually show, and why your environment carries more weight than your DNA, which is exactly the kind of specificity the body's regulatory systems are built around. The body has a thousand built-in ways to file 'self' from 'not self', and they hold steady until they do not. The not-yet quietly can become the now suddenly under enough load. What we are after is not a moral story but a load story, the kind of story you can plot on graph paper and still cry about. Reading the mechanism this way also lets us argue gently with our own panic, which is otherwise our worst guide. The body is not the enemy in this story; it is the witness.

Where the same trigger lands differently in different bodies

Two patients can take the same hit and walk away with very different futures. Some of that is the genetics they were born into; some of it is what their immune system was already busy with at the moment of impact; some of it is the season of life they were in. Reading our own case carefully usually means going back further than feels necessary, sometimes a decade or more, to find the soil the seed met. The autoimmune story is almost never about the moment of diagnosis; it is about the years before the moment, where most of the actual work was done. For readers carrying the version of this story tied to genetic load vs environment, bottom line is the same: this lever is yours; use it slowly, and let the body teach you the dose.

Common questions, answered plainly

Common questions show up around this. Should I get genetic testing? Almost never urgently, the load story carries more weight than the genome story. Should I be afraid of every infection? No, but worth taking the seasonal ones seriously. Should I move house? Only after looking at the room you actually live in. Should I leave the relationship that taught me to brace? That is a different conversation, gentle, and sometimes a true yes. Most weeks the answer to the question is smaller than the question deserved.

Where this fits into the wider picture

This sits beside the other levers. Gut health, sleep, nervous-system tone, food choices, and the relationship with our own life. No single lever does the whole job, and the honest accounting is that the people who recover are the ones who, gently and over years, use four or five of them. The library this article belongs to is built around that arithmetic. You can keep this in mind as you read other pieces in the Immune Rebuilt library; every essay here is meant to be read alongside the others, not as a stand-alone fix.

A small, kind move you can make today

A kind, small move for this week is to write, by hand, a one-page timeline of the year before your symptoms began. Not as a forensic exercise, as a long quiet practice in honesty. The mind keeps a tidy story; the page keeps a more honest one. Then put it down, and read one piece in this library that fits the loudest line on your timeline. That is enough work for this week.

What changes when you adopt the load-story

Adopting the load-story does not mean you stop caring about a specific cause; it means you stop trying to find the single one. The questions you bring to your clinician change. The way you read the labs changes. The way you frame your own story to friends and family changes. The patient who has internalised the load-story tends to make smaller, calmer decisions across a longer arc, and those decisions accumulate in ways the snapshot view of medicine does not capture. Over years, what looked like 'doing nothing' from the outside often turns out to have been the patient and steady tending of half a dozen small fires. The autoimmune body responds to that kind of attention more reliably than to any heroic intervention.

Where this fits with the rest of the autoimmune library

This piece is one window into a larger view, and the rest of the windows are next door. You can read this beside our pieces on gut healing, on the nervous system, on the dietary protocols, on the slower work of grief and pattern. They were written to be read in any order, and they are easier to hold together than apart. The autoimmune story is plural, and the library tries to honour that plurality by being plural itself, not a single argument repeated in different costumes. If a particular piece feels too sharp on a given day, put it down. Pick another. The body knows when it can metabolise a hard idea and when it cannot, and the library will be here either way.

Common questions, answered plainly

How long before I should expect to feel different? Honest answer: most patients in this library report the first soft signal somewhere between week three and week six, a deeper change between month two and month four, and a meaningfully different baseline by month six to nine. Almost nobody who recovers does so in two weeks. Almost everybody who recovers describes a quiet arc rather than a single dramatic shift, and that arc is built from a hundred small days, not three intense ones.

Do I need a clinician for this? For diagnosis and for medication decisions, yes, almost always. For the day-to-day work of nutrition, sleep, nervous-system practice, and gentle movement, the patient is the primary author of the protocol; a good clinician partners on it without trying to take it over. If your current clinician is uninterested in the daily work, it does not mean the daily work is wrong; it means a complementary set of eyes may be useful.

What if I try this and nothing happens? It is the wrong question early. The right early question is whether your sleep, your stool, your daily energy curve, and your social capacity are showing small movements in the right direction. Symptoms are a noisy lagging indicator; the leading indicators are quieter and earlier, and the patients who watch the leading indicators tend to keep going long enough for the symptoms to follow.

Is any of this safe alongside my medication? Almost everything we describe here is safe alongside conventional medication; a few specific botanicals and a few specific dietary changes interact with specific drug classes, and those interactions are worth bringing to the prescribing clinician before starting. In our experience, conventional rheumatology and gentle daily-life work coexist well far more often than they conflict, and the patients who get the benefit of both tend to do better than patients who feel forced to pick a side.

Where does this site stand on conventional medicine? Squarely in favour of it, used well, and squarely in favour of the patient also using the daily levers it does not always discuss. The two are not in competition; they are different chapters of the same recovery, and the recovery goes faster when both chapters are open at once.

What if family or close friends do not understand? This one is among the most common quiet sorrows of autoimmune life. The body is doing real work that other people cannot see, and that invisibility is its own injury. You will not always be able to bring loved ones along, and trying too hard to convince them is a quiet drain on the very energy you need for healing. Pick one or two trusted witnesses, hold the rest at a kind distance, and trust that some of them will come around once results show; some will not, and that is allowed too. The work continues either way, and the work is what changes the body, not the audience around it.

What about flare days, when none of this feels possible? Lower the bar. Drink water. Eat something gentle. Lie down by an open window. Cancel what you can cancel without guilt. Flare days are not the days for new protocols; they are the days for old kindness, the kind you would offer a child with the flu. The protocol you keep on the easy days is the one that quietly carries you through the hard ones.

Where to read more

For readers who want to go beyond this article, the National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases on autoimmune disease is a useful next stop: https://www.niaid.nih.gov/diseases-conditions/autoimmune-diseases. You will not find a single answer there, but you will find the kind of careful summary the field has actually produced, which is more useful than the loud opinions a search will surface first.

Reading on. Root causes are not a single dragon to slay; they are weather to learn. Read this site as a long quiet conversation about that weather, not as a checklist. The Immune Rebuilt library at https://immunerebuilt.com/articles holds the rest of the conversation, and most readers find that one piece a week is the right pace.

A short reader letter

One more thing before you close the tab. The autoimmune body has been listening, all this time, to a louder set of voices than it should have. Marketing voices. Influencer voices. Loud-clinic voices. Worried family voices. The voice this site is trying to add to that crowd is quieter on purpose, because the body in flare hears the quiet voices first. We are not trying to win an argument with the loud ones. We are trying to be the voice you can return to on a hard day, the way you would return to a kind friend who happens to know the territory. Most of the people who recover do so partly because they found one or two such voices and let those voices set the pace. It is genuinely fine if Immune Rebuilt is not one of those voices for you; pick whichever ones are, and stay with them. The point is not the source. The point is the calm.

If you are reading this in a hard week, please put it down for a moment and breathe. None of what follows in the rest of the Immune Rebuilt library is urgent. The library will still be here next week, and the body, given a kind enough environment, will keep doing the patient work it has always been doing. This piece on genetic load vs environment is a small contribution to that environment; please use it the way you would use a quiet conversation with a friend who has read more than you have on this particular question, and who would never tell you what to do.

Many of you write in with versions of the same kind question: where do I begin. The honest answer is that you have already begun, and the work now is to keep beginning, gently, in a way you can sustain past the first month. Root Causes is one room of the larger house this site is trying to describe; keep walking through it slowly, and pick the next room based on the room you are standing in, not the one a search engine showed you first. You can keep this in mind as you read other pieces in the Immune Rebuilt library; every essay here is meant to be read alongside the others, not as a stand-alone fix.

articles.title: The threshold model of autoimmunity
articles.excerpt: What it means that your immune system has a tipping point, and how to give it room.
articles.body:
TL;DR. how this question is usually asked, and why that matters; the honest mechanism, in plain english; where the same trigger lands differently in different bodies; common questions, answered plainly; where this fits into the wider picture; a small, kind move you can make today. The point is to read this slowly, compare it against your own story, and pick one small, kind move for this week.

By The Immune Rebuilt Editorial Team for Immune Rebuilt . May 4, 2026

On the days when nothing else seems to land, this is the lens that still earns its keep, but the answer worth giving in plain English is rarely the one that gets the most clicks. This is part of the Immune Rebuilt library on autoimmune disease, and it tries to be specific, unhurried, and honest about what we do and do not know. What it means that your immune system has a tipping point, and how to give it room.

If you are coming to this fresh, you may also want the wider frame in our piece on lupus and the quiet life, which sets out the lens this site uses across the library, including kids teens and autoimmunity and the multi hit hypothesis, before we narrow the field to root causes.

How this question is usually asked, and why that matters

The shape of the question really does matter. If we ask, with some impatience, what is the one cause of autoimmune disease, the literature shrugs at us, because there is not one. If we ask, instead, what are the recurring environmental and biological loads that, layered on top of a susceptible body, make autoimmune disease more likely, the literature offers a long, careful answer. The first question gets a quick fight on the internet. The second question gets a slow conversation that is, in our experience, where the actual recovery happens. The angle for this piece, what it means that your immune system has a tipping point, and how to give it room, sits inside that second question; the first one is too small a doorway to walk through.

The honest mechanism, in plain English

Mechanism, in plain English, looks like this. In the case of the threshold model of autoimmunity, that machinery is specifically about what it means that your immune system has a tipping point, and how to give it room, which is exactly the kind of specificity the body's regulatory systems are built around. The body has a thousand built-in ways to file 'self' from 'not self', and they hold steady until they do not. The not-yet quietly can become the now suddenly under enough load. What we are after is not a moral story but a load story, the kind of story you can plot on graph paper and still cry about. Reading the mechanism this way also lets us argue gently with our own panic, which is otherwise our worst guide. The body is not the enemy in this story; it is the witness.

Where the same trigger lands differently in different bodies

Two patients can take the same hit and walk away with very different futures. Some of that is the genetics they were born into; some of it is what their immune system was already busy with at the moment of impact; some of it is the season of life they were in. Reading our own case carefully usually means going back further than feels necessary, sometimes a decade or more, to find the soil the seed met. The autoimmune story is almost never about the moment of diagnosis; it is about the years before the moment, where most of the actual work was done. For readers carrying the version of this story tied to the threshold model of autoimmunity, bottom line is the same: this lever is yours; use it slowly, and let the body teach you the dose.

Common questions, answered plainly

Common questions show up around this. Should I get genetic testing? Almost never urgently, the load story carries more weight than the genome story. Should I be afraid of every infection? No, but worth taking the seasonal ones seriously. Should I move house? Only after looking at the room you actually live in. Should I leave the relationship that taught me to brace? That is a different conversation, gentle, and sometimes a true yes. Most weeks the answer to the question is smaller than the question deserved.

Where this fits into the wider picture

This sits beside the other levers. Gut health, sleep, nervous-system tone, food choices, and the relationship with our own life. No single lever does the whole job, and the honest accounting is that the people who recover are the ones who, gently and over years, use four or five of them. The library this article belongs to is built around that arithmetic. You can keep this in mind as you read other pieces in the Immune Rebuilt library; every essay here is meant to be read alongside the others, not as a stand-alone fix.

A small, kind move you can make today

A kind, small move for this week is to write, by hand, a one-page timeline of the year before your symptoms began. Not as a forensic exercise, as a long quiet practice in honesty. The mind keeps a tidy story; the page keeps a more honest one. Then put it down, and read one piece in this library that fits the loudest line on your timeline. That is enough work for this week.

What changes when you adopt the load-story

Adopting the load-story does not mean you stop caring about a specific cause; it means you stop trying to find the single one. The questions you bring to your clinician change. The way you read the labs changes. The way you frame your own story to friends and family changes. The patient who has internalised the load-story tends to make smaller, calmer decisions across a longer arc, and those decisions accumulate in ways the snapshot view of medicine does not capture. Over years, what looked like 'doing nothing' from the outside often turns out to have been the patient and steady tending of half a dozen small fires. The autoimmune body responds to that kind of attention more reliably than to any heroic intervention.

Where this fits with the rest of the autoimmune library

This piece is one window into a larger view, and the rest of the windows are next door. You can read this beside our pieces on gut healing, on the nervous system, on the dietary protocols, on the slower work of grief and pattern. They were written to be read in any order, and they are easier to hold together than apart. The autoimmune story is plural, and the library tries to honour that plurality by being plural itself, not a single argument repeated in different costumes. If a particular piece feels too sharp on a given day, put it down. Pick another. The body knows when it can metabolise a hard idea and when it cannot, and the library will be here either way.

Common questions, answered plainly

How long before I should expect to feel different? Honest answer: most patients in this library report the first soft signal somewhere between week three and week six, a deeper change between month two and month four, and a meaningfully different baseline by month six to nine. Almost nobody who recovers does so in two weeks. Almost everybody who recovers describes a quiet arc rather than a single dramatic shift, and that arc is built from a hundred small days, not three intense ones.

Do I need a clinician for this? For diagnosis and for medication decisions, yes, almost always. For the day-to-day work of nutrition, sleep, nervous-system practice, and gentle movement, the patient is the primary author of the protocol; a good clinician partners on it without trying to take it over. If your current clinician is uninterested in the daily work, it does not mean the daily work is wrong; it means a complementary set of eyes may be useful.

What if I try this and nothing happens? It is the wrong question early. The right early question is whether your sleep, your stool, your daily energy curve, and your social capacity are showing small movements in the right direction. Symptoms are a noisy lagging indicator; the leading indicators are quieter and earlier, and the patients who watch the leading indicators tend to keep going long enough for the symptoms to follow.

Is any of this safe alongside my medication? Almost everything we describe here is safe alongside conventional medication; a few specific botanicals and a few specific dietary changes interact with specific drug classes, and those interactions are worth bringing to the prescribing clinician before starting. In our experience, conventional rheumatology and gentle daily-life work coexist well far more often than they conflict, and the patients who get the benefit of both tend to do better than patients who feel forced to pick a side.

Where does this site stand on conventional medicine? Squarely in favour of it, used well, and squarely in favour of the patient also using the daily levers it does not always discuss. The two are not in competition; they are different chapters of the same recovery, and the recovery goes faster when both chapters are open at once.

What if family or close friends do not understand? This one is among the most common quiet sorrows of autoimmune life. The body is doing real work that other people cannot see, and that invisibility is its own injury. You will not always be able to bring loved ones along, and trying too hard to convince them is a quiet drain on the very energy you need for healing. Pick one or two trusted witnesses, hold the rest at a kind distance, and trust that some of them will come around once results show; some will not, and that is allowed too. The work continues either way, and the work is what changes the body, not the audience around it.

What about flare days, when none of this feels possible? Lower the bar. Drink water. Eat something gentle. Lie down by an open window. Cancel what you can cancel without guilt. Flare days are not the days for new protocols; they are the days for old kindness, the kind you would offer a child with the flu. The protocol you keep on the easy days is the one that quietly carries you through the hard ones.

Where to read more

For readers who want to go beyond this article, the National Institutes of Health overview of the gut microbiome is a useful next stop: https://www.niddk.nih.gov/health-information/digestive-diseases. You will not find a single answer there, but you will find the kind of careful summary the field has actually produced, which is more useful than the loud opinions a search will surface first.

Reading on. There is no single light switch in autoimmune medicine. There are dozens of small ones. Read the library for the rest of them. The Immune Rebuilt library at https://immunerebuilt.com/articles holds the rest of the conversation, and most readers find that one piece a week is the right pace.

A short reader letter

One more thing before you close the tab. The autoimmune body has been listening, all this time, to a louder set of voices than it should have. Marketing voices. Influencer voices. Loud-clinic voices. Worried family voices. The voice this site is trying to add to that crowd is quieter on purpose, because the body in flare hears the quiet voices first. We are not trying to win an argument with the loud ones. We are trying to be the voice you can return to on a hard day, the way you would return to a kind friend who happens to know the territory. Most of the people who recover do so partly because they found one or two such voices and let those voices set the pace. It is genuinely fine if Immune Rebuilt is not one of those voices for you; pick whichever ones are, and stay with them. The point is not the source. The point is the calm.

If you are reading this in a hard week, please put it down for a moment and breathe. None of what follows in the rest of the Immune Rebuilt library is urgent. The library will still be here next week, and the body, given a kind enough environment, will keep doing the patient work it has always been doing. This piece on the threshold model of autoimmunity is a small contribution to that environment; please use it the way you would use a quiet conversation with a friend who has read more than you have on this particular question, and who would never tell you what to do.

Many of you write in with versions of the same kind question: where do I begin. The honest answer is that you have already begun, and the work now is to keep beginning, gently, in a way you can sustain past the first month. Root Causes is one room of the larger house this site is trying to describe; keep walking through it slowly, and pick the next room based on the room you are standing in, not the one a search engine showed you first. You can keep this in mind as you read other pieces in the Immune Rebuilt library; every essay here is meant to be read alongside the others, not as a stand-alone fix.

For the day-to-day work of nutrition, sleep, nervous-system practice, and gentle movement, the patient is the primary author of the protocol; a good clinician partners on it without trying to take it over. If your current clinician is uninterested in the daily work, it does not mean the daily work is wrong; it means a complementary set of eyes may be useful.

What if I try this and nothing happens? It is the wrong question early. The right early question is whether your sleep, your stool, your daily energy curve, and your social capacity are showing small movements in the right direction. Symptoms are a noisy lagging indicator; the leading indicators are quieter and earlier, and the patients who watch the leading indicators tend to keep going long enough for the symptoms to follow.

Is any of this safe alongside my medication? Almost everything we describe here is safe alongside conventional medication; a few specific botanicals and a few specific dietary changes interact with specific drug classes, and those interactions are worth bringing to the prescribing clinician before starting. In our experience, conventional rheumatology and gentle daily-life work coexist well far more often than they conflict, and the patients who get the benefit of both tend to do better than patients who feel forced to pick a side.

Where does this site stand on conventional medicine? Squarely in favour of it, used well, and squarely in favour of the patient also using the daily levers it does not always discuss. The two are not in competition; they are different chapters of the same recovery, and the recovery goes faster when both chapters are open at once.

What if family or close friends do not understand? This one is among the most common quiet sorrows of autoimmune life. The body is doing real work that other people cannot see, and that invisibility is its own injury. You will not always be able to bring loved ones along, and trying too hard to convince them is a quiet drain on the very energy you need for healing. Pick one or two trusted witnesses, hold the rest at a kind distance, and trust that some of them will come around once results show; some will not, and that is allowed too. The work continues either way, and the work is what changes the body, not the audience around it.

What about flare days, when none of this feels possible? Lower the bar. Drink water. Eat something gentle. Lie down by an open window. Cancel what you can cancel without guilt. Flare days are not the days for new protocols; they are the days for old kindness, the kind you would offer a child with the flu. The protocol you keep on the easy days is the one that quietly carries you through the hard ones.

Where to read more

TL;DR. how this question is usually asked, and why that matters; the honest mechanism, in plain english; where the same trigger lands differently in different bodies; common questions, answered plainly; where this fits into the wider picture; a small, kind move you can make today. The point is to read this slowly, compare it against your own story, and pick one small, kind move for this week.

How this question is usually asked, and why that matters

The shape of the question really does matter. If we ask, with some impatience, what is the one cause of autoimmune disease, the literature shrugs at us, because there is not one. If we ask, instead, what are the recurring environmental and biological loads that, layered on top of a susceptible body, make autoimmune disease more likely, the literature offers a long, careful answer. The first question gets a quick fight on the internet. The second question gets a slow conversation that is, in our experience, where the actual recovery happens. The angle for this piece, what the literature actually supports, what it doesn't, and where the supplement crowd gets ahead of the data, sits inside that second question; the first one is too small a doorway to walk through.

The honest mechanism, in plain English

Mechanism, in plain English, looks like this. In the case of vitamin d and autoimmunity, that machinery is specifically about what the literature actually supports, what it doesn't, and where the supplement crowd gets ahead of the data, which is exactly the kind of specificity the body's regulatory systems are built around. The body has a thousand built-in ways to file 'self' from 'not self', and they hold steady until they do not. The not-yet quietly can become the now suddenly under enough load. What we are after is not a moral story but a load story, the kind of story you can plot on graph paper and still cry about. Reading the mechanism this way also lets us argue gently with our own panic, which is otherwise our worst guide. The body is not the enemy in this story; it is the witness.

Where the same trigger lands differently in different bodies

Two patients can take the same hit and walk away with very different futures. Some of that is the genetics they were born into; some of it is what their immune system was already busy with at the moment of impact; some of it is the season of life they were in. Reading our own case carefully usually means going back further than feels necessary, sometimes a decade or more, to find the soil the seed met. The autoimmune story is almost never about the moment of diagnosis; it is about the years before the moment, where most of the actual work was done. For readers carrying the version of this story tied to vitamin d and autoimmunity, bottom line is the same: this lever is yours; use it slowly, and let the body teach you the dose.

Common questions, answered plainly

Common questions show up around this. Should I get genetic testing? Almost never urgently, the load story carries more weight than the genome story. Should I be afraid of every infection? No, but worth taking the seasonal ones seriously. Should I move house? Only after looking at the room you actually live in. Should I leave the relationship that taught me to brace? That is a different conversation, gentle, and sometimes a true yes. Most weeks the answer to the question is smaller than the question deserved.

Where this fits into the wider picture

This sits beside the other levers. Gut health, sleep, nervous-system tone, food choices, and the relationship with our own life. No single lever does the whole job, and the honest accounting is that the people who recover are the ones who, gently and over years, use four or five of them. The library this article belongs to is built around that arithmetic. You can keep this in mind as you read other pieces in the Immune Rebuilt library; every essay here is meant to be read alongside the others, not as a stand-alone fix.
