A small, kind move you can make today

A kind, small move for this week is to write, by hand, a one-page timeline of the year before your symptoms began. Not as a forensic exercise, as a long quiet practice in honesty. The mind keeps a tidy story; the page keeps a more honest one. Then put it down, and read one piece in this library that fits the loudest line on your timeline. That is enough work for this week.

What changes when you adopt the load-story

Adopting the load-story does not mean you stop caring about a specific cause; it means you stop trying to find the single one. The questions you bring to your clinician change. The way you read the labs changes. The way you frame your own story to friends and family changes. The patient who has internalised the load-story tends to make smaller, calmer decisions across a longer arc, and those decisions accumulate in ways the snapshot view of medicine does not capture. Over years, what looked like 'doing nothing' from the outside often turns out to have been the patient and steady tending of half a dozen small fires. The autoimmune body responds to that kind of attention more reliably than to any heroic intervention.

Where this fits with the rest of the autoimmune library

This piece is one window into a larger view, and the rest of the windows are next door. You can read this beside our pieces on gut healing, on the nervous system, on the dietary protocols, on the slower work of grief and pattern. They were written to be read in any order, and they are easier to hold together than apart. The autoimmune story is plural, and the library tries to honour that plurality by being plural itself, not a single argument repeated in different costumes. If a particular piece feels too sharp on a given day, put it down. Pick another. The body knows when it can metabolise a hard idea and when it cannot, and the library will be here either way.

Common questions, answered plainly

How long before I should expect to feel different? Honest answer: most patients in this library report the first soft signal somewhere between week three and week six, a deeper change between month two and month four, and a meaningfully different baseline by month six to nine. Almost nobody who recovers does so in two weeks. Almost everybody who recovers describes a quiet arc rather than a single dramatic shift, and that arc is built from a hundred small days, not three intense ones.

Do I need a clinician for this? For diagnosis and for medication decisions, yes, almost always. For the day-to-day work of nutrition, sleep, nervous-system practice, and gentle movement, the patient is the primary author of the protocol; a good clinician partners on it without trying to take it over. If your current clinician is uninterested in the daily work, it does not mean the daily work is wrong; it means a complementary set of eyes may be useful.

What if I try this and nothing happens? It is the wrong question early. The right early question is whether your sleep, your stool, your daily energy curve, and your social capacity are showing small movements in the right direction. Symptoms are a noisy lagging indicator; the leading indicators are quieter and earlier, and the patients who watch the leading indicators tend to keep going long enough for the symptoms to follow.

Is any of this safe alongside my medication? Almost everything we describe here is safe alongside conventional medication; a few specific botanicals and a few specific dietary changes interact with specific drug classes, and those interactions are worth bringing to the prescribing clinician before starting. In our experience, conventional rheumatology and gentle daily-life work coexist well far more often than they conflict, and the patients who get the benefit of both tend to do better than patients who feel forced to pick a side.

Where does this site stand on conventional medicine? Squarely in favour of it, used well, and squarely in favour of the patient also using the daily levers it does not always discuss. The two are not in competition; they are different chapters of the same recovery, and the recovery goes faster when both chapters are open at once.

What if family or close friends do not understand? This one is among the most common quiet sorrows of autoimmune life. The body is doing real work that other people cannot see, and that invisibility is its own injury. You will not always be able to bring loved ones along, and trying too hard to convince them is a quiet drain on the very energy you need for healing. Pick one or two trusted witnesses, hold the rest at a kind distance, and trust that some of them will come around once results show; some will not, and that is allowed too. The work continues either way, and the work is what changes the body, not the audience around it.

What about flare days, when none of this feels possible? Lower the bar. Drink water. Eat something gentle. Lie down by an open window. Cancel what you can cancel without guilt. Flare days are not the days for new protocols; they are the days for old kindness, the kind you would offer a child with the flu. The protocol you keep on the easy days is the one that quietly carries you through the hard ones.

Where to read more

For readers who want to go beyond this article, the National Psoriasis Foundation clinical resources is a useful next stop: https://www.psoriasis.org/about-psoriasis/. You will not find a single answer there, but you will find the kind of careful summary the field has actually produced, which is more useful than the loud opinions a search will surface first.

Reading on. Causes are plural, and your story is allowed to be specific. Keep reading the library slowly, and let the specifics teach you. The Immune Rebuilt library at https://immunerebuilt.com/articles holds the rest of the conversation, and most readers find that one piece a week is the right pace.

A short reader letter

One more thing before you close the tab. The autoimmune body has been listening, all this time, to a louder set of voices than it should have. Marketing voices. Influencer voices. Loud-clinic voices. Worried family voices. The voice this site is trying to add to that crowd is quieter on purpose, because the body in flare hears the quiet voices first. We are not trying to win an argument with the loud ones. We are trying to be the voice you can return to on a hard day, the way you would return to a kind friend who happens to know the territory. Most of the people who recover do so partly because they found one or two such voices and let those voices set the pace. It is genuinely fine if Immune Rebuilt is not one of those voices for you; pick whichever ones are, and stay with them. The point is not the source. The point is the calm.

If you are reading this in a hard week, please put it down for a moment and breathe. None of what follows in the rest of the Immune Rebuilt library is urgent. The library will still be here next week, and the body, given a kind enough environment, will keep doing the patient work it has always been doing. This piece on circadian rhythm and immune function is a small contribution to that environment; please use it the way you would use a quiet conversation with a friend who has read more than you have on this particular question, and who would never tell you what to do.

Many of you write in with versions of the same kind question: where do I begin. The honest answer is that you have already begun, and the work now is to keep beginning, gently, in a way you can sustain past the first month. Root Causes is one room of the larger house this site is trying to describe; keep walking through it slowly, and pick the next room based on the room you are standing in, not the one a search engine showed you first. Mechanism, in plain English, looks like this. In the case of the gut immune axis overview, that machinery is specifically about gALT, M-cells, sIgA, and why a little vocabulary helps a lot, which is exactly the kind of specificity the body's regulatory systems are built around. The body has a thousand built-in ways to file 'self' from 'not self', and they hold steady until they do not. The not-yet quietly can become the now suddenly under enough load. What we are after is not a moral story but a load story, the kind of story you can plot on graph paper and still cry about. Reading the mechanism this way also lets us argue gently with our own panic, which is otherwise our worst guide. The body is not the enemy in this story; it is the witness.

Where the same trigger lands differently in different bodies

Two patients can take the same hit and walk away with very different futures. Some of that is the genetics they were born into; some of it is what their immune system was already busy with at the moment of impact; some of it is the season of life they were in. Reading our own case carefully usually means going back further than feels necessary, sometimes a decade or more, to find the soil the seed met. The autoimmune story is almost never about the moment of diagnosis; it is about the years before the moment, where most of the actual work was done. For readers carrying the version of this story tied to the gut immune axis overview, bottom line is the same: this lever is yours; use it slowly, and let the body teach you the dose.

Common questions, answered plainly

Common questions show up around this. Should I get genetic testing? Almost never urgently, the load story carries more weight than the genome story. Should I be afraid of every infection? No, but worth taking the seasonal ones seriously. Should I move house? Only after looking at the room you actually live in. Should I leave the relationship that taught me to brace? That is a different conversation, gentle, and sometimes a true yes. Most weeks the answer to the question is smaller than the question deserved.

Where this fits into the wider picture

This sits beside the other levers. Gut health, sleep, nervous-system tone, food choices, and the relationship with our own life. No single lever does the whole job, and the honest accounting is that the people who recover are the ones who, gently and over years, use four or five of them. The library this article belongs to is built around that arithmetic. You can keep this in mind as you read other pieces in the Immune Rebuilt library; every essay here is meant to be read alongside the others, not as a stand-alone fix.

A small, kind move you can make today

A kind, small move for this week is to write, by hand, a one-page timeline of the year before your symptoms began. Not as a forensic exercise, as a long quiet practice in honesty. The mind keeps a tidy story; the page keeps a more honest one. Then put it down, and read one piece in this library that fits the loudest line on your timeline. That is enough work for this week.

What changes when you adopt the load-story

Adopting the load-story does not mean you stop caring about a specific cause; it means you stop trying to find the single one. The questions you bring to your clinician change. The way you read the labs changes. The way you frame your own story to friends and family changes. The patient who has internalised the load-story tends to make smaller, calmer decisions across a longer arc, and those decisions accumulate in ways the snapshot view of medicine does not capture. Over years, what looked like 'doing nothing' from the outside often turns out to have been the patient and steady tending of half a dozen small fires. The autoimmune body responds to that kind of attention more reliably than to any heroic intervention.

Where this fits with the rest of the autoimmune library

This piece is one window into a larger view, and the rest of the windows are next door. You can read this beside our pieces on gut healing, on the nervous system, on the dietary protocols, on the slower work of grief and pattern. They were written to be read in any order, and they are easier to hold together than apart. The autoimmune story is plural, and the library tries to honour that plurality by being plural itself, not a single argument repeated in different costumes. If a particular piece feels too sharp on a given day, put it down. Pick another. The body knows when it can metabolise a hard idea and when it cannot, and the library will be here either way.

Common questions, answered plainly

How long before I should expect to feel different? Honest answer: most patients in this library report the first soft signal somewhere between week three and week six, a deeper change between month two and month four, and a meaningfully different baseline by month six to nine. Almost nobody who recovers does so in two weeks. Almost everybody who recovers describes a quiet arc rather than a single dramatic shift, and that arc is built from a hundred small days, not three intense ones.

Do I need a clinician for this? For diagnosis and for medication decisions, yes, almost always. For the day-to-day work of nutrition, sleep, nervous-system practice, and gentle movement, the patient is the primary author of the protocol; a good clinician partners on it without trying to take it over. If your current clinician is uninterested in the daily work, it does not mean the daily work is wrong; it means a complementary set of eyes may be useful.

What if I try this and nothing happens? It is the wrong question early. The right early question is whether your sleep, your stool, your daily energy curve, and your social capacity are showing small movements in the right direction. Symptoms are a noisy lagging indicator; the leading indicators are quieter and earlier, and the patients who watch the leading indicators tend to keep going long enough for the symptoms to follow.

Is any of this safe alongside my medication? Almost everything we describe here is safe alongside conventional medication; a few specific botanicals and a few specific dietary changes interact with specific drug classes, and those interactions are worth bringing to the prescribing clinician before starting. In our experience, conventional rheumatology and gentle daily-life work coexist well far more often than they conflict, and the patients who get the benefit of both tend to do better than patients who feel forced to pick a side.

Where does this site stand on conventional medicine? Squarely in favour of it, used well, and squarely in favour of the patient also using the daily levers it does not always discuss. The two are not in competition; they are different chapters of the same recovery, and the recovery goes faster when both chapters are open at once.

What if family or close friends do not understand? This one is among the most common quiet sorrows of autoimmune life. The body is doing real work that other people cannot see, and that invisibility is its own injury. You will not always be able to bring loved ones along, and trying too hard to convince them is a quiet drain on the very energy you need for healing. Pick one or two trusted witnesses, hold the rest at a kind distance, and trust that some of them will come around once results show; some will not, and that is allowed too. The work continues either way, and the work is what changes the body, not the audience around it.

What about flare days, when none of this feels possible? Lower the bar. Drink water. Eat something gentle. Lie down by an open window. Cancel what you can cancel without guilt. Flare days are not the days for new protocols; they are the days for old kindness, the kind you would offer a child with the flu. The protocol you keep on the easy days is the one that quietly carries you through the hard ones.

Where to read more

For readers who want to go beyond this article, the Endometriosis Foundation immune research page is a useful next stop: https://www.endofound.org/research. You will not find a single answer there, but you will find the kind of careful summary the field has actually produced, which is more useful than the loud opinions a search will surface first.

Reading on. Causes are plural, and your story is allowed to be specific. Keep reading the library slowly, and let the specifics teach you. The Immune Rebuilt library at https://immunerebuilt.com/articles holds the rest of the conversation, and most readers find that one piece a week is the right pace.

A short reader letter

One more thing before you close the tab. The autoimmune body has been listening, all this time, to a louder set of voices than it should have. Marketing voices. Influencer voices. Loud-clinic voices. Worried family voices. The voice this site is trying to add to that crowd is quieter on purpose, because the body in flare hears the quiet voices first. We are not trying to win an argument with the loud ones. We are trying to be the voice you can return to on a hard day, the way you would return to a kind friend who happens to know the territory. Most of the people who recover do so partly because they found one or two such voices and let those voices set the pace. It is genuinely fine if Immune Rebuilt is not one of those voices for you; pick whichever ones are, and stay with them. The point is not the source. The point is the calm.

If you are reading this in a hard week, please put it down for a moment and breathe. None of what follows in the rest of the Immune Rebuilt library is urgent. The library will still be here next week, and the body, given a kind enough environment, will keep doing the patient work it has always been doing. This piece on the gut immune axis overview is a small contribution to that environment; please use it the way you would use a quiet conversation with a friend who has read more than you have on this particular question, and who would never tell you what to do.

Many of you write in with versions of the same kind question: where do I begin. The honest answer is that you have already begun, and the work now is to keep beginning, gently, in a way you can sustain past the first month. Root Causes is one room of the larger house this site is trying to describe; keep walking through it slowly, and pick the next room based on the room you are standing in, not the one a search engine showed you first. You can keep this in mind as you read other pieces in the Immune Rebuilt library; every essay here is meant to be read alongside the others, not as a stand-alone fix.

articles.title: What childhood infections may have set in motion
articles.excerpt: Why your strep, your mono, your bouts of flu can echo decades later.
articles.body:
TL;DR. how this question is usually asked, and why that matters; the honest mechanism, in plain english; where the same trigger lands differently in different bodies; common questions, answered plainly; where this fits into the wider picture; a small, kind move you can make today. The point is to read this slowly, compare it against your own story, and pick one small, kind move for this week.

By The Immune Rebuilt Editorial Team for Immune Rebuilt . May 15, 2026

Most patients arrive at this question already exhausted by the headline answers, but the answer worth giving in plain English is rarely the one that gets the most clicks. This is part of the Immune Rebuilt library on autoimmune disease, and it tries to be specific, unhurried, and honest about what we do and do not know. Why your strep, your mono, your bouts of flu can echo decades later.

If you are coming to this fresh, you may also want the wider frame in our piece on ebv reactivation and the hpa axis, which sets out the lens this site uses across the library, including aip protocol honest guide and the gut immune axis overview, before we narrow the field to root causes.

How this question is usually asked, and why that matters

The shape of the question really does matter. If we ask, with some impatience, what is the one cause of autoimmune disease, the literature shrugs at us, because there is not one. If we ask, instead, what are the recurring environmental and biological loads that, layered on top of a susceptible body, make autoimmune disease more likely, the literature offers a long, careful answer. The first question gets a quick fight on the internet. The second question gets a slow conversation that is, in our experience, where the actual recovery happens. The angle for this piece, why your strep, your mono, your bouts of flu can echo decades later, sits inside that second question; the first one is too small a doorway to walk through.

The honest mechanism, in plain English

Mechanism, in plain English, looks like this. In the case of childhood infections and adult autoimmunity, that machinery is specifically about why your strep, your mono, your bouts of flu can echo decades later, which is exactly the kind of specificity the body's regulatory systems are built around. The body has a thousand built-in ways to file 'self' from 'not self', and they hold steady until they do not. The not-yet quietly can become the now suddenly under enough load. What we are after is not a moral story but a load story, the kind of story you can plot on graph paper and still cry about. Reading the mechanism this way also lets us argue gently with our own panic, which is otherwise our worst guide. The body is not the enemy in this story; it is the witness.

Where the same trigger lands differently in different bodies

Two patients can take the same hit and walk away with very different futures. Some of that is the genetics they were born into; some of it is what their immune system was already busy with at the moment of impact; some of it is the season of life they were in. Reading our own case carefully usually means going back further than feels necessary, sometimes a decade or more, to find the soil the seed met. The autoimmune story is almost never about the moment of diagnosis; it is about the years before the moment, where most of the actual work was done. For readers carrying the version of this story tied to childhood infections and adult autoimmunity, bottom line is the same: this lever is yours; use it slowly, and let the body teach you the dose.

Common questions, answered plainly

Common questions show up around this. Should I get genetic testing? Almost never urgently, the load story carries more weight than the genome story. Should I be afraid of every infection? No, but worth taking the seasonal ones seriously. Should I move house? Only after looking at the room you actually live in. Should I leave the relationship that taught me to brace? That is a different conversation, gentle, and sometimes a true yes. Most weeks the answer to the question is smaller than the question deserved.

Where this fits into the wider picture

This sits beside the other levers. Gut health, sleep, nervous-system tone, food choices, and the relationship with our own life. No single lever does the whole job, and the honest accounting is that the people who recover are the ones who, gently and over years, use four or five of them. The library this article belongs to is built around that arithmetic. You can keep this in mind as you read other pieces in the Immune Rebuilt library; every essay here is meant to be read alongside the others, not as a stand-alone fix.

A small, kind move you can make today

A kind, small move for this week is to write, by hand, a one-page timeline of the year before your symptoms began. Not as a forensic exercise, as a long quiet practice in honesty. The mind keeps a tidy story; the page keeps a more honest one. Then put it down, and read one piece in this library that fits the loudest line on your timeline. That is enough work for this week.

What changes when you adopt the load-story

Adopting the load-story does not mean you stop caring about a specific cause; it means you stop trying to find the single one. The questions you bring to your clinician change. The way you read the labs changes. The way you frame your own story to friends and family changes. The patient who has internalised the load-story tends to make smaller, calmer decisions across a longer arc, and those decisions accumulate in ways the snapshot view of medicine does not capture. Over years, what looked like 'doing nothing' from the outside often turns out to have been the patient and steady tending of half a dozen small fires. The autoimmune body responds to that kind of attention more reliably than to any heroic intervention.

Where this fits with the rest of the autoimmune library

This piece is one window into a larger view, and the rest of the windows are next door. You can read this beside our pieces on gut healing, on the nervous system, on the dietary protocols, on the slower work of grief and pattern. They were written to be read in any order, and they are easier to hold together than apart. The autoimmune story is plural, and the library tries to honour that plurality by being plural itself, not a single argument repeated in different costumes. If a particular piece feels too sharp on a given day, put it down. Pick another. The body knows when it can metabolise a hard idea and when it cannot, and the library will be here either way.

Common questions, answered plainly

How long before I should expect to feel different? Honest answer: most patients in this library report the first soft signal somewhere between week three and week six, a deeper change between month two and month four, and a meaningfully different baseline by month six to nine. Almost nobody who recovers does so in two weeks. Almost everybody who recovers describes a quiet arc rather than a single dramatic shift, and that arc is built from a hundred small days, not three intense ones.

Do I need a clinician for this? For diagnosis and for medication decisions, yes, almost always. For the day-to-day work of nutrition, sleep, nervous-system practice, and gentle movement, the patient is the primary author of the protocol; a good clinician partners on it without trying to take it over. If your current clinician is uninterested in the daily work, it does not mean the daily work is wrong; it means a complementary set of eyes may be useful.

What if I try this and nothing happens? It is the wrong question early. The right early question is whether your sleep, your stool, your daily energy curve, and your social capacity are showing small movements in the right direction. Symptoms are a noisy lagging indicator; the leading indicators are quieter and earlier, and the patients who watch the leading indicators tend to keep going long enough for the symptoms to follow.

Is any of this safe alongside my medication? Almost everything we describe here is safe alongside conventional medication; a few specific botanicals and a few specific dietary changes interact with specific drug classes, and those interactions are worth bringing to the prescribing clinician before starting. In our experience, conventional rheumatology and gentle daily-life work coexist well far more often than they conflict, and the patients who get the benefit of both tend to do better than patients who feel forced to pick a side.

Where does this site stand on conventional medicine? Squarely in favour of it, used well, and squarely in favour of the patient also using the daily levers it does not always discuss. The two are not in competition; they are different chapters of the same recovery, and the recovery goes faster when both chapters are open at once.

What if family or close friends do not understand? This one is among the most common quiet sorrows of autoimmune life. The body is doing real work that other people cannot see, and that invisibility is its own injury. You will not always be able to bring loved ones along, and trying too hard to convince them is a quiet drain on the very energy you need for healing. Pick one or two trusted witnesses, hold the rest at a kind distance, and trust that some of them will come around once results show; some will not, and that is allowed too. The work continues either way, and the work is what changes the body, not the audience around it.

What about flare days, when none of this feels possible? Lower the bar. Drink water. Eat something gentle. Lie down by an open window. Cancel what you can cancel without guilt. Flare days are not the days for new protocols; they are the days for old kindness, the kind you would offer a child with the flu. The protocol you keep on the easy days is the one that quietly carries you through the hard ones.

Where to read more

For readers who want to go beyond this article, the SAMHSA trauma-informed approach overview is a useful next stop: https://www.samhsa.gov/concept-trauma. You will not find a single answer there, but you will find the kind of careful summary the field has actually produced, which is more useful than the loud opinions a search will surface first.

Reading on. Causes are plural, and your story is allowed to be specific. Keep reading the library slowly, and let the specifics teach you. The Immune Rebuilt library at https://immunerebuilt.com/articles holds the rest of the conversation, and most readers find that one piece a week is the right pace.

A short reader letter

One more thing before you close the tab. The autoimmune body has been listening, all this time, to a louder set of voices than it should have. Marketing voices. Influencer voices. Loud-clinic voices. Worried family voices. The voice this site is trying to add to that crowd is quieter on purpose, because the body in flare hears the quiet voices first. We are not trying to win an argument with the loud ones. We are trying to be the voice you can return to on a hard day, the way you would return to a kind friend who happens to know the territory. Most of the people who recover do so partly because they found one or two such voices and let those voices set the pace. It is genuinely fine if Immune Rebuilt is not one of those voices for you; pick whichever ones are, and stay with them. The point is not the source. The point is the calm.

If you are reading this in a hard week, please put it down for a moment and breathe. None of what follows in the rest of the Immune Rebuilt library is urgent. The library will still be here next week, and the body, given a kind enough environment, will keep doing the patient work it has always been doing. This piece on childhood infections and adult autoimmunity is a small contribution to that environment; please use it the way you would use a quiet conversation with a friend who has read more than you have on this particular question, and who would never tell you what to do.

Many of you write in with versions of the same kind question: where do I begin. The honest answer is that you have already begun, and the work now is to keep beginning, gently, in a way you can sustain past the first month. Root Causes is one room of the larger house this site is trying to describe; keep walking through it slowly, and pick the next room based on the room you are standing in, not the one a search engine showed you first. You can keep this in mind as you read other pieces in the Immune Rebuilt library; every essay here is meant to be read alongside the others, not as a stand-alone fix.

articles.title: The hygiene hypothesis, revisited
articles.excerpt: What the original idea got right, what it got wrong, and where it stands now.
articles.body:
TL;DR. how this question is usually asked, and why that matters; the honest mechanism, in plain english; where the same trigger lands differently in different bodies; common questions, answered plainly; where this fits into the wider picture; a small, kind move you can make today. The point is to read this slowly, compare it against your own story, and pick one small, kind move for this week.

By The Immune Rebuilt Editorial Team for Immune Rebuilt . May 18, 2026

It is fair to say very few questions inside autoimmune medicine attract more advice and less clarity than the question of cause, but the answer worth giving in plain English is rarely the one that gets the most clicks. This is part of the Immune Rebuilt library on autoimmune disease, and it tries to be specific, unhurried, and honest about what we do and do not know. What the original idea got right, what it got wrong, and where it stands now.

If you are coming to this fresh, you may also want the wider frame in our piece on aip protocol honest guide, which sets out the lens this site uses across the library, including men and autoimmune disease and antibiotic exposure as cumulative load, before we narrow the field to root causes.

How this question is usually asked, and why that matters

The shape of the question really does matter. If we ask, with some impatience, what is the one cause of autoimmune disease, the literature shrugs at us, because there is not one. If we ask, instead, what are the recurring environmental and biological loads that, layered on top of a susceptible body, make autoimmune disease more likely, the literature offers a long, careful answer. The first question gets a quick fight on the internet. The second question gets a slow conversation that is, in our experience, where the actual recovery happens. The angle for this piece, what the original idea got right, what it got wrong, and where it stands now, sits inside that second question; the first one is too small a doorway to walk through.

The honest mechanism, in plain English

Mechanism, in plain English, looks like this. In the case of the hygiene hypothesis update, that machinery is specifically about what the original idea got right, what it got wrong, and where it stands now, which is exactly the kind of specificity the body's regulatory systems are built around. The body has a thousand built-in ways to file 'self' from 'not self', and they hold steady until they do not. The not-yet quietly can become the now suddenly under enough load. What we are after is not a moral story but a load story, the kind of story you can plot on graph paper and still cry about. Reading the mechanism this way also lets us argue gently with our own panic, which is otherwise our worst guide. The body is not the enemy in this story; it is the witness.

Where the same trigger lands differently in different bodies

Two patients can take the same hit and walk away with very different futures. Some of that is the genetics they were born into; some of it is what their immune system was already busy with at the moment of impact; some of it is the season of life they were in. Reading our own case carefully usually means going back further than feels necessary, sometimes a decade or more, to find the soil the seed met. The autoimmune story is almost never about the moment of diagnosis; it is about the years before the moment, where most of the actual work was done. For readers carrying the version of this story tied to the hygiene hypothesis update, bottom line is the same: this lever is yours; use it slowly, and let the body teach you the dose.

Common questions, answered plainly

Common questions show up around this. Should I get genetic testing? Almost never urgently, the load story carries more weight than the genome story. Should I be afraid of every infection? No, but worth taking the seasonal ones seriously. Should I move house? Only after looking at the room you actually live in. Should I leave the relationship that taught me to brace? That is a different conversation, gentle, and sometimes a true yes. Most weeks the answer to the question is smaller than the question deserved.

Where this fits into the wider picture

This sits beside the other levers. Gut health, sleep, nervous-system tone, food choices, and the relationship with our own life. No single lever does the whole job, and the honest accounting is that the people who recover are the ones who, gently and over years, use four or five of them. The library this article belongs to is built around that arithmetic. You can keep this in mind as you read other pieces in the Immune Rebuilt library; every essay here is meant to be read alongside the others, not as a stand-alone fix.

A small, kind move you can make today

A kind, small move for this week is to write, by hand, a one-page timeline of the year before your symptoms began. Not as a forensic exercise, as a long quiet practice in honesty. The mind keeps a tidy story; the page keeps a more honest one. Then put it down, and read one piece in this library that fits the loudest line on your timeline. That is enough work for this week.

What changes when you adopt the load-story

Adopting the load-story does not mean you stop caring about a specific cause; it means you stop trying to find the single one. The questions you bring to your clinician change. The way you read the labs changes. The way you frame your own story to friends and family changes. The patient who has internalised the load-story tends to make smaller, calmer decisions across a longer arc, and those decisions accumulate in ways the snapshot view of medicine does not capture. Over years, what looked like 'doing nothing' from the outside often turns out to have been the patient and steady tending of half a dozen small fires. The autoimmune body responds to that kind of attention more reliably than to any heroic intervention.

Where this fits with the rest of the autoimmune library

This piece is one window into a larger view, and the rest of the windows are next door. You can read this beside our pieces on gut healing, on the nervous system, on the dietary protocols, on the slower work of grief and pattern. They were written to be read in any order, and they are easier to hold together than apart. The autoimmune story is plural, and the library tries to honour that plurality by being plural itself, not a single argument repeated in different costumes. If a particular piece feels too sharp on a given day, put it down. Pick another. The body knows when it can metabolise a hard idea and when it cannot, and the library will be here either way.

Common questions, answered plainly

How long before I should expect to feel different? Honest answer: most patients in this library report the first soft signal somewhere between week three and week six, a deeper change between month two and month four, and a meaningfully different baseline by month six to nine. Almost nobody who recovers does so in two weeks. Almost everybody who recovers describes a quiet arc rather than a single dramatic shift, and that arc is built from a hundred small days, not three intense ones.

Do I need a clinician for this? For diagnosis and for medication decisions, yes, almost always. For the day-to-day work of nutrition, sleep, nervous-system practice, and gentle movement, the patient is the primary author of the protocol; a good clinician partners on it without trying to take it over. If your current clinician is uninterested in the daily work, it does not mean the daily work is wrong; it means a complementary set of eyes may be useful.

What if I try this and nothing happens? It is the wrong question early. The right early question is whether your sleep, your stool, your daily energy curve, and your social capacity are showing small movements in the right direction. Symptoms are a noisy lagging indicator; the leading indicators are quieter and earlier, and the patients who watch the leading indicators tend to keep going long enough for the symptoms to follow.

Is any of this safe alongside my medication? Almost everything we describe here is safe alongside conventional medication; a few specific botanicals and a few specific dietary changes interact with specific drug classes, and those interactions are worth bringing to the prescribing clinician before starting. In our experience, conventional rheumatology and gentle daily-life work coexist well far more often than they conflict, and the patients who get the benefit of both tend to do better than patients who feel forced to pick a side.

Where does this site stand on conventional medicine? Squarely in favour of it, used well, and squarely in favour of the patient also using the daily levers it does not always discuss. The two are not in competition; they are different chapters of the same recovery, and the recovery goes faster when both chapters are open at once.

What if family or close friends do not understand? This one is among the most common quiet sorrows of autoimmune life. The body is doing real work that other people cannot see, and that invisibility is its own injury. You will not always be able to bring loved ones along, and trying too hard to convince them is a quiet drain on the very energy you need for healing. Pick one or two trusted witnesses, hold the rest at a kind distance, and trust that some of them will come around once results show; some will not, and that is allowed too. The work continues either way, and the work is what changes the body, not the audience around it.

What about flare days, when none of this feels possible? Lower the bar. Drink water. Eat something gentle. Lie down by an open window. Cancel what you can cancel without guilt. Flare days are not the days for new protocols; they are the days for old kindness, the kind you would offer a child with the flu. The protocol you keep on the easy days is the one that quietly carries you through the hard ones.

Where to read more

For readers who want to go beyond this article, the EPA mercury health-effects page is a useful next stop: https://www.epa.gov/mercury/health-effects-exposures-mercury. You will not find a single answer there, but you will find the kind of careful summary the field has actually produced, which is more useful than the loud opinions a search will surface first.

Reading on. Causes are plural, and your story is allowed to be specific. Keep reading the library slowly, and let the specifics teach you. The Immune Rebuilt library at https://immunerebuilt.com/articles holds the rest of the conversation, and most readers find that one piece a week is the right pace.

A short reader letter

One more thing before you close the tab. The autoimmune body has been listening, all this time, to a louder set of voices than it should have. Marketing voices. Influencer voices. Loud-clinic voices. Worried family voices. The voice this site is trying to add to that crowd is quieter on purpose, because the body in flare hears the quiet voices first. We are not trying to win an argument with the loud ones. We are trying to be the voice you can return to on a hard day, the way you would return to a kind friend who happens to know the territory. Most of the people who recover do so partly because they found one or two such voices and let those voices set the pace. It is genuinely fine if Immune Rebuilt is not one of those voices for you; pick whichever ones are, and stay with them. The point is not the source. The point is the calm.

If you are reading this in a hard week, please put it down for a moment and breathe. None of what follows in the rest of the Immune Rebuilt library is urgent. The library will still be here next week, and the body, given a kind enough environment, will keep doing the patient work it has always been doing. This piece on the hygiene hypothesis update is a small contribution to that environment; please use it the way you would use a quiet conversation with a friend who has read more than you have on this particular question, and who would never tell you what to do.

Many of you write in with versions of the same kind question: where do I begin. The honest answer is that you have already begun, and the work now is to keep beginning, gently, in a way you can sustain past the first month. Do I need a clinician for this? For diagnosis and for medication decisions, yes, almost always. For the day-to-day work of nutrition, sleep, nervous-system practice, and gentle movement, the patient is the primary author of the protocol; a good clinician partners on it without trying to take it over. If your current clinician is uninterested in the daily work, it does not mean the daily work is wrong; it means a complementary set of eyes may be useful.

What if I try this and nothing happens? It is the wrong question early. The right early question is whether your sleep, your stool, your daily energy curve, and your social capacity are showing small movements in the right direction. Symptoms are a noisy lagging indicator; the leading indicators are quieter and earlier, and the patients who watch the leading indicators tend to keep going long enough for the symptoms to follow.

Is any of this safe alongside my medication? Almost everything we describe here is safe alongside conventional medication; a few specific botanicals and a few specific dietary changes interact with specific drug classes, and those interactions are worth bringing to the prescribing clinician before starting. In our experience, conventional rheumatology and gentle daily-life work coexist well far more often than they conflict, and the patients who get the benefit of both tend to do better than patients who feel forced to pick a side.

Where does this site stand on conventional medicine? Squarely in favour of it, used well, and squarely in favour of the patient also using the daily levers it does not always discuss. The two are not in competition; they are different chapters of the same recovery, and the recovery goes faster when both chapters are open at once.

What if family or close friends do not understand? This one is among the most common quiet sorrows of autoimmune life. The body is doing real work that other people cannot see, and that invisibility is its own injury. You will not always be able to bring loved ones along, and trying too hard to convince them is a quiet drain on the very energy you need for healing. Pick one or two trusted witnesses, hold the rest at a kind distance, and trust that some of them will come around once results show; some will not, and that is allowed too. The work continues either way, and the work is what changes the body, not the audience around it.

What about flare days, when none of this feels possible? Lower the bar. Drink water. Eat something gentle. Lie down by an open window. Cancel what you can cancel without guilt. Flare days are not the days for new protocols; they are the days for old kindness, the kind you would offer a child with the flu. The protocol you keep on the easy days is the one that quietly carries you through the hard ones.

Where to read more

articles.title: HLA genes, plain English
articles.excerpt: The polymorphisms that show up over and over, and what to do with that information.
articles.body:
TL;DR. how this question is usually asked, and why that matters; the honest mechanism, in plain english; where the same trigger lands differently in different bodies; common questions, answered plainly; where this fits into the wider picture; a small, kind move you can make today. The point is to read this slowly, compare it against your own story, and pick one small, kind move for this week.

If you are coming to this fresh, you may also want the wider frame in our piece on gi map and stool testing, which sets out the lens this site uses across the library, including mold and the environment question and estrogen and autoimmune prevalence, before we narrow the field to root causes.

How this question is usually asked, and why that matters

The shape of the question really does matter. If we ask, with some impatience, what is the one cause of autoimmune disease, the literature shrugs at us, because there is not one. If we ask, instead, what are the recurring environmental and biological loads that, layered on top of a susceptible body, make autoimmune disease more likely, the literature offers a long, careful answer. The first question gets a quick fight on the internet. The second question gets a slow conversation that is, in our experience, where the actual recovery happens. The angle for this piece, the polymorphisms that show up over and over, and what to do with that information, sits inside that second question; the first one is too small a doorway to walk through.

The honest mechanism, in plain English

Mechanism, in plain English, looks like this. In the case of hla genes and autoimmune disease, that machinery is specifically about the polymorphisms that show up over and over, and what to do with that information, which is exactly the kind of specificity the body's regulatory systems are built around. The body has a thousand built-in ways to file 'self' from 'not self', and they hold steady until they do not. The not-yet quietly can become the now suddenly under enough load. What we are after is not a moral story but a load story, the kind of story you can plot on graph paper and still cry about. Reading the mechanism this way also lets us argue gently with our own panic, which is otherwise our worst guide. The body is not the enemy in this story; it is the witness.

Where the same trigger lands differently in different bodies

Two patients can take the same hit and walk away with very different futures. Some of that is the genetics they were born into; some of it is what their immune system was already busy with at the moment of impact; some of it is the season of life they were in. Reading our own case carefully usually means going back further than feels necessary, sometimes a decade or more, to find the soil the seed met. The autoimmune story is almost never about the moment of diagnosis; it is about the years before the moment, where most of the actual work was done. For readers carrying the version of this story tied to hla genes and autoimmune disease, bottom line is the same: this lever is yours; use it slowly, and let the body teach you the dose.

Common questions, answered plainly

Common questions show up around this. Should I get genetic testing? Almost never urgently, the load story carries more weight than the genome story. Should I be afraid of every infection? No, but worth taking the seasonal ones seriously. Should I move house? Only after looking at the room you actually live in. Should I leave the relationship that taught me to brace? That is a different conversation, gentle, and sometimes a true yes. Most weeks the answer to the question is smaller than the question deserved.

Where this fits into the wider picture

This sits beside the other levers. Gut health, sleep, nervous-system tone, food choices, and the relationship with our own life. No single lever does the whole job, and the honest accounting is that the people who recover are the ones who, gently and over years, use four or five of them. The library this article belongs to is built around that arithmetic. You can keep this in mind as you read other pieces in the Immune Rebuilt library; every essay here is meant to be read alongside the others, not as a stand-alone fix.

You can keep this in mind as you read other pieces in the Immune Rebuilt library; every essay here is meant to be read alongside the others, not as a stand-alone fix.

A small, kind move you can make today

A kind, small move for this week is to write, by hand, a one-page timeline of the year before your symptoms began. Not as a forensic exercise, as a long quiet practice in honesty. The mind keeps a tidy story; the page keeps a more honest one. Then put it down, and read one piece in this library that fits the loudest line on your timeline. That is enough work for this week.

What changes when you adopt the load-story

Adopting the load-story does not mean you stop caring about a specific cause; it means you stop trying to find the single one. The questions you bring to your clinician change. The way you read the labs changes. The way you frame your own story to friends and family changes. The patient who has internalised the load-story tends to make smaller, calmer decisions across a longer arc, and those decisions accumulate in ways the snapshot view of medicine does not capture. Over years, what looked like 'doing nothing' from the outside often turns out to have been the patient and steady tending of half a dozen small fires. The autoimmune body responds to that kind of attention more reliably than to any heroic intervention.

Where this fits with the rest of the autoimmune library

This piece is one window into a larger view, and the rest of the windows are next door. You can read this beside our pieces on gut healing, on the nervous system, on the dietary protocols, on the slower work of grief and pattern. They were written to be read in any order, and they are easier to hold together than apart. The autoimmune story is plural, and the library tries to honour that plurality by being plural itself, not a single argument repeated in different costumes. If a particular piece feels too sharp on a given day, put it down. Pick another. The body knows when it can metabolise a hard idea and when it cannot, and the library will be here either way.

Common questions, answered plainly

How long before I should expect to feel different? Honest answer: most patients in this library report the first soft signal somewhere between week three and week six, a deeper change between month two and month four, and a meaningfully different baseline by month six to nine. Almost nobody who recovers does so in two weeks. Almost everybody who recovers describes a quiet arc rather than a single dramatic shift, and that arc is built from a hundred small days, not three intense ones.

Do I need a clinician for this? For diagnosis and for medication decisions, yes, almost always. For the day-to-day work of nutrition, sleep, nervous-system practice, and gentle movement, the patient is the primary author of the protocol; a good clinician partners on it without trying to take it over. If your current clinician is uninterested in the daily work, it does not mean the daily work is wrong; it means a complementary set of eyes may be useful.

What if I try this and nothing happens? It is the wrong question early. The right early question is whether your sleep, your stool, your daily energy curve, and your social capacity are showing small movements in the right direction. Symptoms are a noisy lagging indicator; the leading indicators are quieter and earlier, and the patients who watch the leading indicators tend to keep going long enough for the symptoms to follow.

Is any of this safe alongside my medication? Almost everything we describe here is safe alongside conventional medication; a few specific botanicals and a few specific dietary changes interact with specific drug classes, and those interactions are worth bringing to the prescribing clinician before starting. In our experience, conventional rheumatology and gentle daily-life work coexist well far more often than they conflict, and the patients who get the benefit of both tend to do better than patients who feel forced to pick a side.

Where does this site stand on conventional medicine? Squarely in favour of it, used well, and squarely in favour of the patient also using the daily levers it does not always discuss. The two are not in competition; they are different chapters of the same recovery, and the recovery goes faster when both chapters are open at once.

What if family or close friends do not understand? This one is among the most common quiet sorrows of autoimmune life. The body is doing real work that other people cannot see, and that invisibility is its own injury. You will not always be able to bring loved ones along, and trying too hard to convince them is a quiet drain on the very energy you need for healing. Pick one or two trusted witnesses, hold the rest at a kind distance, and trust that some of them will come around once results show; some will not, and that is allowed too. The work continues either way, and the work is what changes the body, not the audience around it.

What about flare days, when none of this feels possible? Lower the bar. Drink water. Eat something gentle. Lie down by an open window. Cancel what you can cancel without guilt. Flare days are not the days for new protocols; they are the days for old kindness, the kind you would offer a child with the flu. The protocol you keep on the easy days is the one that quietly carries you through the hard ones.

Where to read more

For readers who want to go beyond this article, the NIH page on the vagus nerve and the inflammatory reflex is a useful next stop: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3134032/. You will not find a single answer there, but you will find the kind of careful summary the field has actually produced, which is more useful than the loud opinions a search will surface first.

Reading on. Root causes are not a single dragon to slay; they are weather to learn. Read this site as a long quiet conversation about that weather, not as a checklist. A short reader letter

Most of the people who recover do so partly because they found one or two such voices and let those voices set the pace. It is genuinely fine if Immune Rebuilt is not one of those voices for you; pick whichever ones are, and stay with them. The point is not the source. The point is the calm.

If you are reading this in a hard week, please put it down for a moment and breathe. None of what follows in the rest of the Immune Rebuilt library is urgent. The library will still be here next week, and the body, given a kind enough environment, will keep doing the patient work it has always been doing. This piece on the old friends hypothesis is a small contribution to that environment; please use it the way you would use a quiet conversation with a friend who has read more than you have on this particular question, and who would never tell you what to do.

The honest answer is that you have already begun, and the work now is to keep beginning, gently, in a way you can sustain past the first month. Root Causes is one room of the larger house this site is trying to describe; keep walking through it slowly, and pick the next room based on the room you are standing in, not the one a search engine showed you first. The angle for this piece, what this balance is and why so many flares look like a tilt toward inflammation, sits inside that second question; the first one is too small a doorway to walk through.

The honest mechanism, in plain English

Mechanism, in plain English, looks like this. In the case of th17 and treg balance, that machinery is specifically about what this balance is and why so many flares look like a tilt toward inflammation, which is exactly the kind of specificity the body's regulatory systems are built around. The body has a thousand built-in ways to file 'self' from 'not self', and they hold steady until they do not. The not-yet quietly can become the now suddenly under enough load. What we are after is not a moral story but a load story, the kind of story you can plot on graph paper and still cry about. Reading the mechanism this way also lets us argue gently with our own panic, which is otherwise our worst guide. The body is not the enemy in this story; it is the witness.

Where the same trigger lands differently in different bodies

Two patients can take the same hit and walk away with very different futures. Some of that is the genetics they were born into; some of it is what their immune system was already busy with at the moment of impact; some of it is the season of life they were in. Reading our own case carefully usually means going back further than feels necessary, sometimes a decade or more, to find the soil the seed met. The autoimmune story is almost never about the moment of diagnosis; it is about the years before the moment, where most of the actual work was done. For readers carrying the version of this story tied to th17 and treg balance, bottom line is the same: this lever is yours; use it slowly, and let the body teach you the dose.

Common questions, answered plainly

Common questions show up around this. Should I get genetic testing? Almost never urgently, the load story carries more weight than the genome story. Should I be afraid of every infection? No, but worth taking the seasonal ones seriously. Should I move house? Only after looking at the room you actually live in. Should I leave the relationship that taught me to brace? That is a different conversation, gentle, and sometimes a true yes. Most weeks the answer to the question is smaller than the question deserved.

Where this fits into the wider picture

This sits beside the other levers. Gut health, sleep, nervous-system tone, food choices, and the relationship with our own life. No single lever does the whole job, and the honest accounting is that the people who recover are the ones who, gently and over years, use four or five of them. The library this article belongs to is built around that arithmetic. You can keep this in mind as you read other pieces in the Immune Rebuilt library; every essay here is meant to be read alongside the others, not as a stand-alone fix.

A small, kind move you can make today

A kind, small move for this week is to write, by hand, a one-page timeline of the year before your symptoms began. Not as a forensic exercise, as a long quiet practice in honesty. The mind keeps a tidy story; the page keeps a more honest one. Then put it down, and read one piece in this library that fits the loudest line on your timeline. That is enough work for this week.

What changes when you adopt the load-story

Adopting the load-story does not mean you stop caring about a specific cause; it means you stop trying to find the single one. The questions you bring to your clinician change. The way you read the labs changes. The way you frame your own story to friends and family changes. The patient who has internalised the load-story tends to make smaller, calmer decisions across a longer arc, and those decisions accumulate in ways the snapshot view of medicine does not capture. Over years, what looked like 'doing nothing' from the outside often turns out to have been the patient and steady tending of half a dozen small fires. The autoimmune body responds to that kind of attention more reliably than to any heroic intervention.

Where this fits with the rest of the autoimmune library

This piece is one window into a larger view, and the rest of the windows are next door. You can read this beside our pieces on gut healing, on the nervous system, on the dietary protocols, on the slower work of grief and pattern. They were written to be read in any order, and they are easier to hold together than apart. The autoimmune story is plural, and the library tries to honour that plurality by being plural itself, not a single argument repeated in different costumes. If a particular piece feels too sharp on a given day, put it down. Pick another. The body knows when it can metabolise a hard idea and when it cannot, and the library will be here either way.

Common questions, answered plainly

How long before I should expect to feel different? Honest answer: most patients in this library report the first soft signal somewhere between week three and week six, a deeper change between month two and month four, and a meaningfully different baseline by month six to nine. Almost nobody who recovers does so in two weeks. Almost everybody who recovers describes a quiet arc rather than a single dramatic shift, and that arc is built from a hundred small days, not three intense ones.

Do I need a clinician for this? For diagnosis and for medication decisions, yes, almost always. For the day-to-day work of nutrition, sleep, nervous-system practice, and gentle movement, the patient is the primary author of the protocol; a good clinician partners on it without trying to take it over. If your current clinician is uninterested in the daily work, it does not mean the daily work is wrong; it means a complementary set of eyes may be useful.

What if I try this and nothing happens? It is the wrong question early. The right early question is whether your sleep, your stool, your daily energy curve, and your social capacity are showing small movements in the right direction. Symptoms are a noisy lagging indicator; the leading indicators are quieter and earlier, and the patients who watch the leading indicators tend to keep going long enough for the symptoms to follow.

Is any of this safe alongside my medication? Almost everything we describe here is safe alongside conventional medication; a few specific botanicals and a few specific dietary changes interact with specific drug classes, and those interactions are worth bringing to the prescribing clinician before starting. In our experience, conventional rheumatology and gentle daily-life work coexist well far more often than they conflict, and the patients who get the benefit of both tend to do better than patients who feel forced to pick a side.

Where does this site stand on conventional medicine? Squarely in favour of it, used well, and squarely in favour of the patient also using the daily levers it does not always discuss. The two are not in competition; they are different chapters of the same recovery, and the recovery goes faster when both chapters are open at once.

What if family or close friends do not understand? This one is among the most common quiet sorrows of autoimmune life. The body is doing real work that other people cannot see, and that invisibility is its own injury. You will not always be able to bring loved ones along, and trying too hard to convince them is a quiet drain on the very energy you need for healing. Pick one or two trusted witnesses, hold the rest at a kind distance, and trust that some of them will come around once results show; some will not, and that is allowed too. The work continues either way, and the work is what changes the body, not the audience around it.

What about flare days, when none of this feels possible? Lower the bar. Drink water. Eat something gentle. Lie down by an open window. Cancel what you can cancel without guilt. Flare days are not the days for new protocols; they are the days for old kindness, the kind you would offer a child with the flu. The protocol you keep on the easy days is the one that quietly carries you through the hard ones.

Where to read more

For readers who want to go beyond this article, the American Thyroid Association patient information is a useful next stop: https://www.thyroid.org/thyroid-information/. You will not find a single answer there, but you will find the kind of careful summary the field has actually produced, which is more useful than the loud opinions a search will surface first.

Reading on. Root causes are not a single dragon to slay; they are weather to learn. Read this site as a long quiet conversation about that weather, not as a checklist. The Immune Rebuilt library at https://immunerebuilt.com/articles holds the rest of the conversation, and most readers find that one piece a week is the right pace.

A short reader letter

One more thing before you close the tab. The autoimmune body has been listening, all this time, to a louder set of voices than it should have. Marketing voices. Influencer voices. Loud-clinic voices. Worried family voices. The voice this site is trying to add to that crowd is quieter on purpose, because the body in flare hears the quiet voices first. We are not trying to win an argument with the loud ones. We are trying to be the voice you can return to on a hard day, the way you would return to a kind friend who happens to know the territory. Most of the people who recover do so partly because they found one or two such voices and let those voices set the pace. It is genuinely fine if Immune Rebuilt is not one of those voices for you; pick whichever ones are, and stay with them. The point is not the source. The point is the calm.

If you are reading this in a hard week, please put it down for a moment and breathe. None of what follows in the rest of the Immune Rebuilt library is urgent. The library will still be here next week, and the body, given a kind enough environment, will keep doing the patient work it has always been doing. This piece on th17 and treg balance is a small contribution to that environment; please use it the way you would use a quiet conversation with a friend who has read more than you have on this particular question, and who would never tell you what to do.

Many of you write in with versions of the same kind question: where do I begin. The honest answer is that you have already begun, and the work now is to keep beginning, gently, in a way you can sustain past the first month. Root Causes is one room of the larger house this site is trying to describe; keep walking through it slowly, and pick the next room based on the room you are standing in, not the one a search engine showed you first. You can keep this in mind as you read other pieces in the Immune Rebuilt library; every essay here is meant to be read alongside the others, not as a stand-alone fix.
